custom-ai.summary:
AMS2 Custom AI Drivers (feature added in game V1.3.0.0, Nov 27 2021; current game is 1.6.x in 2026) is an XML-based (NOT JSON) per-vehicle-class driver override system. CRITICAL PATH CORRECTION: the files do NOT live under Documents\Automobilista 2 — they go in the GAME INSTALL directory: <Steam>\steamapps\common\Automobilista 2\UserData\CustomAIDrivers\. One .xml file per vehicle class, named exactly after the class's internal name (e.g. F-Classic_Gen3.xml, F-V10_Gen1.xml, GT3.xml). Documents\Automobilista 2 holds savegames/setups/replays instead.

FILE FORMAT (confirmed against the official Reiza forum spec thread and the JAXB annotations in FitzHastings/AMS2CustomDriversUtil source): root element <custom_ai_drivers>; each entry is <driver livery_name="..."> where livery_name is an XML ATTRIBUTE (case-sensitive, must exactly match the livery's display name in the vehicle-select screen); all other fields are CHILD ELEMENTS: <name> (free text driver name), <country> (3-letter code for flag), and floats 0.0–1.0: <race_skill>, <qualifying_skill>, <aggression>, <defending>, <stamina>, <consistency>, <start_reactions>, <wet_skill>, <tyre_management>, <fuel_management> (oval tracks only), <blue_flag_conceding>, <weather_tyre_changes>, <avoidance_of_mistakes>, <avoidance_of_forced_mistakes>, <vehicle_reliability> (can exceed 0–1/be negative; Reiza gives a mean-minutes-between-failures mapping ~ sqrt((x-y)/(z-y)); >0.6 ≈ reliable). V1.3.5.0 added the two mistake fields; V1.4.0.0 added fuel_management and vehicle_reliability; V1.6.9.8 (2025) added per-livery performance/BoP fields usable in these same files: weight_scalar, power_scalar, drag_scalar (~0.900–1.100; these affect the PLAYER's car too when driving that livery — key for period-correct car performance spread) plus AI setup-bias fields setup_downforce / setup_downforce_randomness. Omitted fields fall back to the game's default value for that livery's stock driver; only the liveries you list are overridden. Semantics: race_skill is compressed around the Opponent Skill slider (e.g. at 90% difficulty, 1.0→~95%, 0.0→~85%); aggression is scaled by the Opponent Aggression setting and at Max everyone is forced to 1.0 (overriding the file); consistency LOW = more variance; most values are calibrated so 0.5 = realistic average.

PER-TRACK OVERRIDES (added V1.3.4.0): duplicate a <driver> entry with an extra attribute tracks="Track_Id1,Track_Id2" (comma-separated internal track ids, e.g. Azure_Circuit_2021, Long_Beach, Monza_1971, Spa_Francorchamps_1993, Silverstone_1975, Interlagos_1991, Hockenheim_1988, Imola_GP_1988, Montreal_Historic); attributes present override the class-wide entry at those tracks, missing ones inherit. This enables per-round form (street-circuit specialists, etc.).

LIVERY/SKIN BINDING: community skin packs use AMS2's official custom-livery system (added V1.0.6.2): DDS textures + an override XML in <install>\Vehicles\Textures\CustomLiveries\Overrides\, structure <USER_OVERRIDES><LIVERY_OVERRIDE LIVERY="51" NAME="Mustang Sampling #5" BASELIVERY="Default"><TEXTURE NAME="BODY" PATH="...dds"/><PREVIEWIMAGE PATH="...dds"/></LIVERY_OVERRIDE></USER_OVERRIDES>. The NAME attribute there defines the livery's display name, and the custom-AI file's livery_name must match it exactly — that is the entire binding mechanism between skin packs and custom AI. Livery slot IDs are discoverable via the -showliveryIDs launch option. Gotchas: matching is case-sensitive; special characters (e.g. ã) and XML-unescaped & cause failures; files should be UTF-8.

LIMITATIONS: works in ALL single-player modes including custom races and the built-in (custom) championship mode; explicitly DISABLED in multiplayer. No JSON; no hot per-race UI — grids are whatever liveries are selected. Championship mode itself (why serious historical players skip it): opponents are filled from the class's livery pool with no way to hand-pick the exact AI entry list per round; duplicate liveries appear when grid size exceeds livery count and identical cars for all opponents are impossible; many scoring presets exist but NO custom points system (no era rules like dropped scores/best-N results, shared drives, fastest-lap point variants); no mid-season driver/team changes, no reliability-driven attrition bookkeeping, no carry-over careers; max ~47 AI opponents (track dependent), AI difficulty 70–120 plus Low/Medium/High/Max aggression. Hence the community pattern the app targets: run single custom races + external season/standings tracking, with community 'season packs' = skin pack (CustomLiveries overrides) + per-class custom AI XML (many packs, e.g. F1 season packs, ship multiple alternative XMLs switched by tools like the 'XML Selector'). Key ecosystem tools: NAMeS / NAMeS+AI real-driver files (OverTake), AMS2 Custom Drivers Utility (GitHub FitzHastings/AMS2CustomDriversUtil — ships machine-readable libraries of all class names, livery names and track xml_names), AMS2 Content Manager (AMS2CM) for installing packs. Open-wheel class file names (from the utility's vehicle library, AMS2 1.5.6.3 era): F-Vintage_Gen1, F-Vintage_Gen2, F-Retro_Gen1/Gen2/Gen3, F-Classic_Gen1..Gen4, F-HiTech_Gen1/Gen2, F-V12, F-V10_Gen1/Gen2, F-Reiza, F-Ultimate, F-Ultimate_Gen2, F-USA_Gen1/Gen2/Gen3, F-USA_2023, F-Junior, F-Inter, F-3, F-Vee, F-Trainer, F-Trainer_A (note 1.6 added more classes not in that 1.5.6.3 list, e.g. F-HiTech Gen3/mid-2000s F1 added around V1.6.9.8).
key_facts: Custom AI folder is in the GAME INSTALL dir, not Documents: C:\Program Files (x86)\Steam\steamapps\common\Automobilista 2\UserData\CustomAIDrivers\ (create if missing)
Format is XML, not JSON. One file per vehicle class, filename = internal class name + .xml (e.g. F-Classic_Gen3.xml, F-V10_Gen1.xml, GT3.xml); names are case-sensitive

Root element: <custom_ai_drivers>; entries: <driver livery_name="..."> ... </driver>; livery_name and tracks are XML attributes, all stats are child elements (exact names verified from JAXB annotations in FitzHastings/AMS2CustomDriversUtil XMLDriver.java/XMLGrid.java)

Exact field names: name, country (3-letter code), race_skill, qualifying_skill, aggression, defending, stamina, consistency, start_reactions, wet_skill, tyre_management, fuel_management (ovals only), blue_flag_conceding, weather_tyre_changes, avoidance_of_mistakes, avoidance_of_forced_mistakes, vehicle_reliability — all floats 0.0–1.0 (vehicle_reliability may go outside 0–1; sqrt((x-y)/(z-y)) mapping from mean minutes between failures, >0.6 = reliable)

V1.6.9.8 (2025) added to the same files: weight_scalar, power_scalar, drag_scalar (~0.900–1.100, BoP-style, also affects the PLAYER when driving that livery) and setup_downforce, setup_downforce_randomness (0.0–1.0, AI setup bias)

Per-track overrides (since V1.3.4.0): repeat a <driver> with tracks="Azure_Circuit_2021,Long_Beach" attribute (comma-separated internal track ids); unspecified fields inherit from the class-wide entry

race_skill is compressed around the Opponent Skill slider (at 90%: 1.0→~95%, 0.0→~85%); aggression is scaled by Opponent Aggression setting and at Max is forced to 1.0 for everyone; low consistency = more lap-time variance; omitted fields keep the stock driver's defaults; only listed liveries are overridden

Livery binding: custom skins live in <install>\Vehicles\Textures\CustomLiveries\Overrides\ as DDS + override XML with <USER_OVERRIDES><LIVERY_OVERRIDE LIVERY="51" NAME="Mustang Sampling #5" BASELIVERY="Default"><TEXTURE NAME="BODY" PATH="...dds"/></LIVERY_OVERRIDE>; custom-AI livery_name must exactly match that NAME string (case-sensitive); livery slot IDs visible with -showliveryIDs launch option

Custom AI works in all single-player modes incl. custom championship; disabled in multiplayer; feature added in game V1.3.0.0 (Nov 27 2021), mistake fields V1.3.5.0, fuel_management+vehicle_reliability V1.4.x

Built-in championship mode limitations: cannot hand-pick specific AI liveries/entry list (random fill from class, duplicate liveries when grid > livery count), cannot run identical cars for all opponents, many points presets but NO custom scoring (no dropped scores/era rules), no mid-season changes or carry-over, max ~47 AI opponents (track-dependent), AI difficulty 70–120

Open-wheel class xml_names (from utility's ams2_vehicles_1.5.6.3.xml): F-Vintage_Gen1/Gen2, F-Retro_Gen1/Gen2/Gen3, F-Classic_Gen1/Gen2/Gen3/Gen4, F-HiTech_Gen1/Gen2, F-V12, F-V10_Gen1/Gen2, F-Reiza, F-Ultimate, F-Ultimate_Gen2, F-USA_Gen1/Gen2/Gen3, F-USA_2023, F-Junior, F-Inter, F-3, F-Vee, F-Trainer, F-Trainer_A

Machine-readable class/livery/track libraries for the app: github.com/FitzHastings/AMS2CustomDriversUtil library/vehicles/ams2_vehicles_1.5.6.3.xml (vehicle_class name + xml_name + <livery> names) and library/tracks/ams2_tracks_1.5.6.3.xml (track name + xml_name + is_oval/is_rx)

Ecosystem tools the app should interoperate with: NAMeS and NAMeS+AI real-driver XML packs (overtake.gg 50989 / 60634), AMS2 Custom Drivers Utility (overtake.gg 61336), AMS2 Content Manager (AMS2CM) for pack installs, 'XML Selector' tool used by F1 season packs to swap per-season custom-AI XMLs
Gotchas for generated files: UTF-8 encoding, XML-escape &, exact case-sensitive livery names (copy from override XML), special characters like ã in stock livery names are problematic
uncertainties: Exact vehicle_reliability formula (sqrt((x-y)/(z-y)) with mean-minutes-between-failures, and the negative-value variant) came from a WebFetch summary of the Reiza thread; wording is paraphrased and per-class min/max failure constants must be read from the thread's spoiler tables directly
Whether the custom-AI XML is re-read on every session load vs only at game start (community edits between sessions suggest per-session load, but no authoritative statement found)

Precise value semantics/allowed range of weight_scalar/power_scalar/drag_scalar (0.900–1.100 reported; one NAMeS+AI user reported seeing no effect in early tests) — verify in-game on current 1.6.x build

The 1.5.6.3 class/track libraries in AMS2CustomDriversUtil predate the 1.6 content drops (e.g. mid-2000s F1 classes added ~V1.6.9.8); current internal class xml_names for 1.6-era classes need re-extraction from a current install

Whether custom liveries folder also resolves under Documents\Automobilista 2 (one OverTake post claimed Documents\Automobilista 2\Vehicles\Textures\CustomLiveries\Overrides; the official Reiza thread points to the game install folder — install folder is the safe canonical location)
Exact list of championship scoring presets ('very large variety' per Fandom wiki, no custom scoring) — full preset enumeration not captured because fandom.com pages returned HTTP 402 to fetches

game-content.summary:
AMS2 v1.6.9.8+ (early 2026; 62 track locations, 210 layouts incl. 44 historic; ~260 cars in 100+ classes) — full inventory for historical-season mapping.

=== OPEN-WHEEL F1-ERA CLASSES (in-game class names AFTER the v1.6.9 renames of Nov/Dec 2025) ===
• F-Vintage Gen1 (1966-67, 3.0L era): fictional F-Vintage Gen1 Model1 (Cosworth-DFV-V8 style, ≈Lotus 49) and Model2 (≈Repco-V8 Brabham style). Base game.
• F-Vintage Gen2 (1969-70): fictional Gen2 Model1/Model2 + licensed Brabham BT26A (1969) and Lotus 49C (1970). Base game.
• F-Retro Gen1 (1974): fictional F-Retro Gen1 V8 (DFV kit-car) and Gen1 V12 (works-V12 style); licensed Lotus 72E (1973-74), McLaren M23 (1974), Brabham BT44 (1974). Base game.
• F-Retro Gen2 (1978-80 ground effect): fictional F-Retro Gen2 (≈Lotus 79 style); licensed Lotus 79 (1978), Brabham BT46B "fan car" (1978), Brabham BT49 (1980, Brazilian Racing Legends DLC).
• F-Retro Gen3 (1983 flat-bottom): fictional F-Retro Gen3 DFY (Cosworth DFY V8) and Gen3 Turbo (BMW-turbo style); licensed McLaren MP4/1C (1983), Brabham BT52 (1983, Brazilian Racing Legends DLC).
• F-Classic Gen1 (1986, ~1300hp turbos): G1M1 (≈Williams FW11 Honda), G1M2 (≈Benetton B186 BMW); licensed Lotus 98T (1986, Brazilian Racing Legends DLC).
• F-Classic Gen2 (1988, turbo-vs-3.5L-atmo split): G2M1 (top NA, ≈Williams FW12/Benetton B188), G2M2 (midfield NA), G2M3 (non-McLaren turbo, ≈Lotus 100T/Ferrari style); licensed McLaren MP4/4 (1988, BRL DLC). Community 1988 skin packs map M1=Williams/Benetton, M2=Ligier/Minardi/AGS/Rial/Coloni/EuroBrun, M3=Lotus/Ferrari/Zakspeed/Arrows.
• F-Classic Gen3 (1990): G3M1 (manual V12 725hp ≈Ferrari 641), G3M2 (sequential V8 632hp ≈Benetton B190), G3M3 (manual V10 734hp ≈Williams FW13B); some listings show an M4; licensed McLaren MP4/5B (1990, BRL DLC).
• F-Classic Gen4 (1991): G4M1 (V12 ≈Ferrari 643), G4M2 (V10 ≈Williams FW14), G4M3 (V8 H-pattern ≈Benetton B191); licensed McLaren MP4/6 (1991, BRL DLC).
• F-HiTech Gen1 (1992, driver-aids era; "Formula HiTech" DLC £5.89): G1M1-M4 fictional (bases incl. Ferrari F92A, Williams FW14B — one variant WITH active suspension/TC, one without, Benetton B192) + licensed McLaren MP4/7A.
• F-HiTech Gen2 (1993; same DLC): G2M1-M3 + licensed McLaren MP4/8. Simulates active suspension, TC, ABS, launch control, auto gearbox.
• Formula Edge (1995; class renamed from F-V12 in v1.6.9.5, free base): F-Edge Model1 = old F-V12 (tractable, powerful), Model2 V10 (twitchy, faster in right hands), Model3 V8 (lighter on fuel) — mirrors 1995's Ferrari V12 / Renault V10 / Ford V8 engine split.
• F-V10 Gen1 (1997-98): fictional F-V10 G1 + licensed McLaren MP4/12 (1997). Base game.
• F-V10 Gen2 (~2000-01): fictional F-V10 G2. Base game.
• F-V10 Gen3 (2005; NEW in v1.6.9.8, free): licensed Renault R25 + fictional F-V10 Gen3 Bridgestone / F-V10 Gen3 Michelin (tire-war variants).
• F-V8 Gen1 (2006; NEW in v1.6.9.8, free): licensed Renault R26 + F-V8 Gen1 Bridgestone / Michelin variants.
• F-V8 Gen2 (2008; NEW in v1.6.9.8, free): licensed Renault R28 + F-V8 Gen2 M1.
• F-V8 Gen3 (2011-13 V8 era): RENAMED from "Formula Reiza" in v1.6.9. Base game.
• F-Hybrid Gen1 (2016 hybrid, ~1000hp; NEW in v1.6.9.5, free).
• F-Hybrid Gen2 (2019): RENAMED from "F-Ultimate Gen1".
• F-Hybrid Gen3 (2022 ground-effect): RENAMED from "F-Ultimate Gen2". Reiza said remaining formula generation gaps to be addressed from Q1 2026; default in-game championships exist for F-V10 Gen3 and F-V8 Gen1/2.

=== JUNIOR LADDER / OTHER OPEN-WHEEL ===
• Karts (base): Kart 4-Stroke Rental GX390, Kart 4-Stroke Race GX390, Kart 2-Stroke 125cc Direct, Kart 2-Stroke 125cc Shifter, Superkart 250cc; Kartcross (dirt, Adrenaline Pt1).
• F-Vee Brasil: F-Vee Gen1 (2011-19, air-cooled VW Fusca engine) + F-Vee Gen1 Fin; F-Vee Gen2 (2025 spec, added v1.6.7.2).
• F-Trainer / F-Trainer Advanced (Formula-First/F1600-style trainers).
• F-Junior (1962 Formula Junior; the car is a licensed Lotus 22).
• F-Inter (Fórmula Inter MG-15, modern Brazilian F4-level).
• F-3 Brazil: Dallara F301 (2001-09 spec) and Dallara F309.
• F-Dirt (dirt-oval midget-style, Adrenaline Pt1).
• F-USA (CART/IndyCar; each historic car has Road Course / Short Oval / Speedway aero configs): Gen1 = 1995 CART (Lola T95/00 Ford-Cosworth & Mercedes; Reynard 95i Ford/Honda/Mercedes) — Racin' USA Pt2 DLC; Gen2 = 1998 CART (Lola T98/00 Ford; Reynard 98i Ford/Honda/Mercedes/Toyota; Swift 009c Ford) — Pt2; Gen3 = 2000 CART (Lola B2K/00 Ford/Mercedes/Toyota; Reynard 2Ki Ford/Honda/Mercedes/Toyota) — Pt2; F-USA 2023 (fictional Dallara IR-18-style) — Racin' USA Pt3.

=== HISTORIC TRACK LAYOUTS (exact layout names) ===
Free/base: Adelaide Historic 1988; Adelaide Modern; Montreal Historic 1988 + Montréal (modern); Kyalami Historic 1976 + Kyalami 2020; Imola Historic 1972 / 1988 / 2001 + Imola (2018+); Interlagos Historic 1976, Interlagos Historic 1978 Outer, Interlagos GP; Jacarepaguá Historic 1988 / 2005 / 2005 Oval / 2012 SCB / 2012 Short; Spielberg Historic 1974 / Historic 1977 (Österreichring) + Spielberg modern; Buenos Aires Circuito No.6 / 6S / 7 / 8 / 9 / 12 / 15; Azure Circuit (=Monaco, fictionalized name, added v1.3); Kansai GP / East / West / Classic (=fictionalized Suzuka; Classic is the historic variant); Oulton Park Classic; Pocono Oval + 6 road/oval combos (added free v1.6.9); Cascais (=Estoril modern) + Cascais Alternate; Brands Hatch GP/Indy; Donington GP/National; Cadwell Park; Snetterton 100/200/300; VIR (5 layouts); Mosport (Canadian Tire Motorsport Park); Goiânia, Londrina, Tarumã, Velopark 2010/2017, Velo Cittá, Brasília, Curitiba, Cascavel, Guaporé, Santa Cruz do Sul, Campo Grande, Curvelo, Salvador street, Galeão Airport, Termas de Río Hondo, Córdoba (3), Ibarra (Ecuador); kart venues (Interlagos/Londrina/Ortona/Speedland/Granja Viana/Buskerud).
Premium Track Pack (single-track packs): Spa Pack — Spa 1970, Spa 1970 1000km, Spa 1993, Spa 2020, Spa 2022; Monza Pack — Monza Historic 1971 (GP), 1971 Junior, 1971 10k combined-with-banked-oval, 1971 10k No Chicane, Monza Historic 1991, Monza modern + Junior; Silverstone Pack — Historic 1975 (+No Chicane), Historic 1991, Historic 2001 (+International/National 2001), modern GP/International/National; Hockenheim Pack — Historic 1977, Historic 1988 (+1988 Short), Historic 2001, modern + National + Short A/B; Nürburgring Pack — Historic 1971 Nordschleife / Südschleife / Gesamtstrecke / Betonschleife + modern GP/Sprint/Veedol/Nordschleife/24h; Barcelona Pack — modern GP (+No Chicane, National).
Historical Track Pack Pt1: Bathurst 1983, Cascais 1988 (Estoril), Jerez 1988 (Jerez also has modern Moto/Chicane layouts, base). Pt2: Barcelona 1991, Montreal Historic 1991, Interlagos 1991, Interlagos 1993. Pt3 (v1.6.8.5, Oct 2025): Le Mans 24h 2005 ("Circuit Du Mans 2005"), Road Atlanta 2005 (+Short), Spa-Francorchamps 2005 (+2005 EC endurance). Pt4 (v1.6.9.8): Barcelona 1999, Barcelona 2001, Imola 2005, Indianapolis 2001 (F1 infield+oval-section GP layout), Spielberg 2001 (A1-Ring), Monza 2005.
Other DLC tracks: Hungaroring (own DLC, v1.6.9.8, modern 4.38km); Le Mans Pack (Circuit des 24 Heures modern + Bugatti); Nürburgring 2025 Pack (GP 2025, Nordschleife 2025, 24h 2025, Tourist); IMSA Track Pack (Sebring 4 layouts, Road Atlanta modern, Canadian Tire Motorsport Park); Racin' USA Pt1: Daytona (Tri-Oval/Road/NASCAR Road), Laguna Seca, Long Beach; Pt2: Watkins Glen (GP/Short, each ±Inner Loop), Road America (+Bend), Cleveland GP (airport); Pt3: Indianapolis Oval + Road Course, Auto Club Speedway (Fontana) Oval + Sports Car Course, World Wide Technology Raceway (Gateway) Oval + Road Long/Short. Adrenaline packs add RX/dirt (Ascurra, Foz, Tykki, Barcelona RX, Spa RX, Hockenheim RX, Nürburgring RX) and STT stadium-truck layouts.
Season-calendar coverage note (community consensus): fully/near-fully period-correct seasons possible for 1967, 1969/70, 1974, 1978, 1983, 1986, 1988, 1990, 1991, 1992, 1993, 1995, 1997, 2001, 2005, 2006, 2008, 2016, 2019, 2022+ using above classes; missing venues for perfect calendars include Zandvoort, Anderstorp, Dijon, Detroit/Phoenix/Dallas/Vegas, Magny-Cours, Suzuka pre-Kansai era names, Mexico, Melbourne.

=== NON-OPEN-WHEEL (bonus configurable series; class names) ===
Stock Car Brasil: Opala 1979, Opala 1986, Omega 1999, Cruze+Corolla classes for 2019/2020/2021/2022/2023/2024. Stock USA Gen1/Gen2/Gen3/Gen3 LM (NASCAR-style, "Racin' USA Bonus Pack", Dec 2025, free for Racin' USA owners). Super V8 (Australian Supercars ZB-style). GT: GT1 (1997-98: McLaren F1 GTR Longtail, Mercedes CLK LM, Nissan R390, Porsche 911 GT1-98), GT1 2005 (DBR9, C5-R, Viper GTS-R, MC12, Murcielago R-GT), GT2 2005 (996 GT3 RSR, Milano GT36), GTR 2004 (BMW M3 E46 GTR), GT Classics (1971-74 Corvette C3.R, 911 RSR 3.0), GT Open, GT3 (2015-19, 6 cars), GT3 Gen2 (2022-23, 8 cars), GT4 (9 cars), GT5, GTE (4 cars). Prototypes: Group C (Sauber C9, Porsche 962C, Nissan R89C, Corvette GTP), LMDh/GTP 2023 (Porsche 963, Cadillac V-Series.R, BMW M Hybrid V8, Alpine A424, Lamborghini SC63, Aston Valkyrie LMH), DPi (Cadillac DPi-V.R), LMP2 Gen1/Gen2 (Oreca 07, Ligier JS P217), LMP1 2005 (Audi R8, Courage C60, Dallara SP1), LMP2 2005 (Lola B05/40), Brazilian P1 Gen1/Gen2, P2, P3, P4 (Metalmoro AJR/MRX, Ginetta G58, Sigma P1, Ligier JS P320, MCR S2000, Roco 001). Touring: Group A (1992 DTM: BMW M3 Sport Evo, 190E Evo2, Audi V8 quattro), M1 Procar (1979), Vintage Touring Tier1 (1973)/Tier2 (1962-65 Mini, Lotus 23), Mini JCW, TSI Cup, Lancer Cup, Sprint Race, Copa Classic B/FL, Old Stock Race, Hot Cars, Copa Uno, Copa Fusca, Copa Montana. Trucks: Copa Truck (Iveco/MAN/Mercedes/VW/Vulkan), Super Trophy Trucks. One-make cups: Porsche Cup 3.8/4.0 (Carrera Cup), Ginetta G40 Cup, Ginetta G55 Supercup, Lamborghini Super Trofeo EVO2, Diablo SV-R Super Trofeo, Ligier European Series (JS2 R, JS P4), Caterham Academy/Supersport/Superlight/620R, Aussie Racing Cars. Rallycross (DS3 RX, Polo RX, Lancer RX, Countryman RX). Road: Supercars/Hypercars/Street classes.

Career-relevant: Reiza's own official AMS2 Career Mode is planned for 2026 (Dec 2025 dev update) — differentiator to plan around. McLaren license is in doubt (OverTake Nov 2025 report), so MP4-series cars may not remain purchasable; Renault R25/R26/R28 were released free partly for this reason.
key_facts: v1.6.9 class RENAMES (current in-game names): Formula Reiza -> F-V8 Gen3 (2011-13 era); F-Ultimate Gen1 -> F-Hybrid Gen2 (2019); F-Ultimate Gen2 -> F-Hybrid Gen3 (2022); F-V12 -> Formula Edge (1995, now 3 models: M1 V12=old F-V12, M2 V10, M3 V8)

v1.6.9.5 added F-Hybrid Gen1 (2016 spec, ~1000hp) free; v1.6.9.8 added F-V10 Gen3 (2005, Renault R25 + Bridgestone/Michelin fictional variants), F-V8 Gen1 (2006, Renault R26 + variants), F-V8 Gen2 (2008, Renault R28 + M1) free

Class->season map: F-Vintage Gen1=1966-67, Gen2=1969-70; F-Retro Gen1=1974, Gen2=1978-80, Gen3=1983; F-Classic Gen1=1986, Gen2=1988, Gen3=1990, Gen4=1991; F-HiTech Gen1=1992, Gen2=1993 (DLC); F-Edge=1995; F-V10 Gen1=1997-98, Gen2=~2000-01, Gen3=2005; F-V8 Gen1=2006, Gen2=2008, Gen3=2011-13; F-Hybrid Gen1=2016, Gen2=2019, Gen3=2022

Real-car bases (community consensus): FC-G1M1=Williams FW11, G1M2=Benetton B186; FC-G2M3=turbo (Lotus/Ferrari-style), G2M1/M2=3.5L atmo; FC-G3: M1 V12=Ferrari 641, M2 V8 seq=Benetton B190, M3 V10=Williams FW13B; FC-G4: M1 V12=Ferrari 643, M2 V10=Williams FW14, M3 V8 H-pattern=Benetton B191; F-HiTech G1 models based on Ferrari F92A / Williams FW14B (one model with active+TC, one without) / Benetton B192; F-Vintage G1M1=Lotus 49-like

Licensed real F1 cars: Lotus 49C, 72E, 79, 98T; Brabham BT26A, BT44, BT46B, BT49, BT52; McLaren M23, MP4/1C, MP4/4, MP4/5B, MP4/6, MP4/7A, MP4/8, MP4/12; Renault R25, R26, R28. Brazilian Racing Legends DLC = BT49, BT52, 98T, MP4/4, MP4/5B, MP4/6; Formula HiTech DLC = 9 cars (G1M1-4, G2M1-3, MP4/7A, MP4/8)

result-capture.summary:
Post-race auto-import for an AMS2 (v1.6.x, Madness engine) companion app is fully feasible; four options investigated, ranked below.

(1) SECOND MONITOR (Winzarten) — BEST turnkey option. Actively maintained (v9.39.0 as of 2026) timing app, GitLab-hosted (gitlab.com/winzarten/SecondMonitor; the GitHub Winzarten/SecondMonitor repo is a stale mirror). Fully supports AMS2 via PC2-format shared memory ("works out of the box; enable shared memory inside AMS 2 settings, in Project Cars 2 format"). In Settings -> Reports you enable per-session export (Race/Qualification/Practice) plus "Also Export .Json File"; it then writes a per-session JSON report to C:\Users\<user>\Documents\SecondMonitor\Reports (XLSX reports get suffix _Line when the player crosses the finish line and _End when the player exits the session). The JSON contains the complete field: verified schema from a real AMS2 48-car report: top-level keys SessionRunTime, SessionType ("Race"), Simulator ("AMS 2"), SessionLengthType, TotalNumberOfLaps, SessionLength, SessionRunDuration, TrackInfo{TrackName, TrackLayoutName, LayoutLength}, Drivers[], IsMultiClass, SessionGuid, PitStops, FuelConsumptionInformation, WasGreen; each Drivers[] entry has DriverId, DriverLongName, ClassName, ClassId, FinishingPosition, FinishingPositionInClass, InitialPosition, InitialPositionInClass, AveragePosition, CarName, TotalLaps, TotalDistance, GapToPlayerRelative, GapToPlayerByTiming, LapsDifferenceToPlayer, TopSpeed, Laps[] (LapNumber, LapTime, sector times, IsValid), IsPlayer, Finished, FinishStatus, Rating. All AI participants with names, finishing order, lap times and DNF status are included. Proven precedent: two community championship trackers already import these JSONs — "Simple Endurance Championship Manager" (overtake.gg/downloads/simple-endurance-championship-manager.74733/) and Rea3070/Endurace-Championship-Manager-AMS2-LMU (Python; reads data["Drivers"], driver["DriverLongName"], driver["FinishingPositionInClass"], driver["FinishStatus"], data["TrackInfo"]["TrackName"], data["SessionRunTime"]). Your .NET app would just FileSystemWatcher the Reports folder. Risks: user must run Second Monitor during the race; JSON schema is app-internal (no formal spec, could change between versions).

(2) DIRECT SHARED MEMORY ($pcars2$) — BEST zero-dependency option, recommended as primary with SM-JSON as fallback/alternative. AMS2 exposes the Project CARS 2 shared memory API via memory-mapped file named "$pcars2$" when Options->System->Shared Memory = "Project CARS 2". Struct (SharedMemory.h, CREST2-AMS2 repo, SHARED_MEMORY_VERSION = 14): mVersion, mBuildVersionNumber, volatile mSequenceNumber (odd while being written — spin until even+unchanged for a consistent snapshot); mNumParticipants; ParticipantInfo mParticipantInfo[64] {mIsActive, mName[64], mRacePosition, mLapsCompleted, mCurrentLap, mCurrentSector, mCurrentLapDistance, mWorldPosition}; parallel arrays [64]: mRaceStates (RACESTATE_INVALID/NOT_STARTED/RACING/FINISHED/DISQUALIFIED/RETIRED/DNF), mFastestLapTimes, mLastLapTimes, mCurrentSector1/2/3Times, mFastestSector1/2/3Times, mLapsInvalidated, mPitModes, mPitSchedules, mCarNames[64][64], mCarClassNames[64][64], mNationalities, mSpeeds; session fields mSessionState (SESSION_PRACTICE/TEST/QUALIFY/FORMATION_LAP/RACE/TIME_ATTACK), mRaceState, mGameState, mLapsInEvent, mSessionAdditionalLaps, mEventTimeRemaining, mTrackLocation, mTrackVariation, mTranslatedTrackLocation, mTrackLength, mSessionDuration. STORED_PARTICIPANTS_MAX=64, STRING_LENGTH_MAX=64. CUSTOM AI NAMES ARE VISIBLE: participant mName reflects UserData\CustomAIDrivers XML names — confirmed by Crew Chief speaking NAMeS-modded driver names read from shared memory, and by Second Monitor AMS2 reports containing real custom-AI driver names. End-of-race capture recipe (proven by diegocbarboza/AMS2_SessionLogger, C++ + nlohmann/json): poll ~1-10 Hz; when mRaceState transitions to RACESTATE_INVALID from non-invalid while previous mSessionState was SESSION_RACE or SESSION_QUALIFY, snapshot the last valid frame; sort participants by mRacePosition; per-driver capture Name, Position, BestLap (mFastestLapTimes), RaceState (finished/DNF/DSQ), CarName, Class — it writes logs/SESSION_RACE_<dd-MM-yyyy_HH-mm-ss>.json. Snapshot on every frame and keep the last one where data was valid, because the classification is wiped when the player exits to menu. In .NET: MemoryMappedFile.OpenExisting("$pcars2$", MemoryMappedFileRights.Read) + Marshal.PtrToStructure or unsafe struct read; CrewChiefV4 (open source, C#) contains a working PC2/AMS2 struct marshalling implementation to copy. Caveat: 64-participant cap (AMS2 grids max ~32-48 offline, so fine); struct version can bump with game updates (v1.6-era Crew Chief note: "AMS2 - updated to latest shared memory version (includes IMO tyre temps and support for extra lap after timed races)"); UDP "Project CARS 2" protocol also exists (participant names arrive in ParticipantsData packets, 16 names/packet) but is strictly more work than the mmap for a same-PC app.

(3) CREST2-AMS2 (viper4gh) — viable but adds a process; best for prototyping. Windows console app serving the same shared memory as JSON over HTTP at http://localhost:8180/crest2/v1/api (port configurable via command line). Query params select sections: ?participants=true&timings=true&eventInformation=true (also buildInfo, gameStates, vehicleInformation, flags, pitInfo, carState, carDamage, weather, wheelsAndTyres...; ?formatted=true pretty-prints). Last release v1.0.1 (2024-01-26); bundles SM headers v6-v13/14; still works with 1.6-era data but is a third-party dependency your users must launch, and it lags game updates. Not worth it when the .NET app can read $pcars2$ directly.

(4) NATIVE EXPORT — DOES NOT EXIST for offline AMS2. Reiza forum confirms no built-in save/export of offline race results (threads 18219, 30902). The userdata\LOG\Results path suggested by Reiza staff applies to AMS1 (rFactor-based), not AMS2. AMS2's dedicated server has result logging but that is multiplayer-only, irrelevant for single-player custom races. Simresults.net does not support AMS2 offline results for the same reason.

FEASIBILITY RANKING for a lightweight .NET desktop app: 1st = built-in $pcars2$ shared-memory reader with end-of-session snapshot (no third-party runtime dependency, custom AI names + full classification + best laps available, ~200 lines; port AMS2_SessionLogger logic / reuse CrewChiefV4 C# structs). 2nd = Second Monitor JSON report watcher (near-zero code, richest data incl. every lap of every driver, gaps, sector times, pit stops — but requires user to install/run Second Monitor and schema is unversioned). Offering BOTH is cheap: same result model, two importers. 3rd = CREST2-AMS2 HTTP polling (only if you want out-of-process isolation). 4th = manual entry fallback (always keep it; shared memory misses sessions where the app wasn't running). Note for the tycoon layer: the itch.io project "The Paddock / Apex Racing Manager" (j-mangaliso.itch.io/thepaddock) already does exactly this loop — writes UserData/CustomAIDrivers XML grids before the race, detects P/Q/R sessions via live shared memory, feeds results into a career sim — proving the whole pipeline (and it is a competitor to study).
key_facts: Second Monitor JSON reports path: C:\Users\<user>\Documents\SecondMonitor\Reports; enable in Settings -> Reports: session-type export checkbox (e.g. Race) + 'Also Export .Json File'

Second Monitor JSON top-level keys: SessionRunTime, SessionType, Simulator ('AMS 2'), SessionLengthType, TotalNumberOfLaps, SessionLength, SessionRunDuration, TrackInfo{TrackName,TrackLayoutName,LayoutLength}, Drivers[], IsMultiClass, SessionGuid, PitStops, FuelConsumptionInformation, WasGreen

Second Monitor Drivers[] entry fields: DriverId, DriverLongName, ClassName, ClassId, FinishingPosition, FinishingPositionInClass, InitialPosition, InitialPositionInClass, AveragePosition, CarName, TotalLaps, TotalDistance, GapToPlayerRelative, GapToPlayerByTiming, LapsDifferenceToPlayer, TopSpeed, Laps[]{LapNumber,LapTime,sectors,IsValid}, IsPlayer, Finished, FinishStatus, Rating

Second Monitor: active at v9.39.0 (2026), source at gitlab.com/winzarten/SecondMonitor, docs/downloads at winzarten.gitlab.io/SecondMonitor; AMS2 supported via 'Project CARS 2' shared memory format; XLSX report suffixes _Line (player crosses line) and _End (player exits session)

AMS2 shared memory: memory-mapped file name $pcars2$; enable Options->System->Shared Memory = 'Project CARS 2'; struct version SHARED_MEMORY_VERSION = 14 in CREST2-AMS2 SharedMemory.h; volatile mSequenceNumber is odd while game is writing (spin until even for consistent read)

Shared memory participant data: mNumParticipants; ParticipantInfo mParticipantInfo[64] {mIsActive, mName[64], mRacePosition, mLapsCompleted, mCurrentLap, mCurrentSector}; arrays[64]: mRaceStates, mFastestLapTimes, mLastLapTimes, mCurrentSector1/2/3Times, mFastestSector1/2/3Times, mLapsInvalidated, mPitModes, mCarNames[64][64], mCarClassNames[64][64], mNationalities; STORED_PARTICIPANTS_MAX=64

Race finish detection enums: RaceState {RACESTATE_INVALID, NOT_STARTED, RACING, FINISHED, DISQUALIFIED, RETIRED, DNF}; SessionState {SESSION_INVALID, PRACTICE, TEST, QUALIFY, FORMATION_LAP, RACE, TIME_ATTACK}; session/track fields mLapsInEvent, mSessionAdditionalLaps, mEventTimeRemaining, mTrackLocation, mTrackVariation, mTranslatedTrackLocation

Custom AI names (UserData\CustomAIDrivers\<Vehicle Class Name>.xml, e.g. 'Group C.xml') DO propagate into shared memory mName — Crew Chief pronounces NAMeS-modded names read from shared memory; Second Monitor AMS2 reports show custom real driver names (e.g. 'Matt Campbell' at Daytona)

Reference implementation for end-of-session JSON dump: github.com/diegocbarboza/AMS2_SessionLogger (C++, nlohmann/json) — detects mRaceState transition to RACESTATE_INVALID after SESSION_RACE/SESSION_QUALIFY, sorts by position, writes logs/SESSION_RACE_<timestamp>.json with Name, Position, BestLap, RaceState, CarName, Class, LapsInEvent, SessionDuration, TrackLocation, TrackVariation, TrackLength
C# shared-memory reading precedent: CrewChiefV4 (open source) marshals the PC2/AMS2 struct; .NET call: MemoryMappedFile.OpenExisting("$pcars2$", MemoryMappedFileRights.Read)

CREST2-AMS2 (github.com/viper4gh/CREST2-AMS2): HTTP JSON bridge at http://localhost:8180/crest2/v1/api, sections via query params (participants, timings, eventInformation, gameStates, flags, pitInfo, carState, weather, ...; ?formatted=true); last release v1.0.1 (2024-01-26)

No native AMS2 offline results export exists (Reiza forum threads 18219 'Offline Race Results - Are they saved?' and 30902 'Export result to XL'); userdata\LOG\Results applies to AMS1 only; AMS2 dedicated-server result logs are multiplayer-only

Existing consumers of Second Monitor JSON proving the import pattern: 'Simple Endurance Championship Manager' (OverTake.gg download 74733) and github.com/Rea3070/Endurace-Championship-Manager-AMS2-LMU (reads data['Drivers'], DriverLongName, FinishingPositionInClass, FinishStatus, InitialPositionInClass, CarName, TrackInfo.TrackName, SessionRunTime)

Direct competitor/prior art for the career-tycoon loop: 'The Paddock / Apex Racing Manager' (j-mangaliso.itch.io/thepaddock) — writes CustomAIDrivers XML grids pre-race, auto-detects P/Q/R sessions via shared memory, feeds results into career sim with points/prize money

AMS2 as of 2026 is v1.6.x (v1.6.9.5 release thread on forum.reizastudios.com); Crew Chief changelog v4.16.0.1: 'AMS2 - updated to latest shared memory version (includes IMO tyre temps and support for extra lap after timed races)'

UDP alternative exists (UDP Protocol Version 'Project CARS 2', configurable frequency) and carries participant names in ParticipantsData packets (16 names per packet), but the $pcars2$ mmap is simpler for a same-PC app

Recommended architecture: primary = in-app $pcars2$ poller with last-valid-frame snapshot at session end; secondary = FileSystemWatcher on Documents\SecondMonitor\Reports for Second Monitor JSON; always keep manual entry as fallback
uncertainties: Exact AMS2 1.6.x shared memory version number in 2026 (CREST2-AMS2 header says SHARED_MEMORY_VERSION=14, but Reiza has bumped the version without always updating public headers before; verify mVersion at runtime and tolerate newer values)
Second Monitor JSON report filename pattern in the Reports folder (example repo files were user-renamed; likely includes session type/track/timestamp but unverified)
Whether Second Monitor's JSON schema is stable across its frequent releases — it is an internal format with no versioning guarantee

Whether CREST2-AMS2 v1.0.1 exposes fields added after Jan 2024 (e.g. 1.6-era additions like IMO tyre temps); its participant/timings sections needed for results predate those additions and should be unaffected

Whether qualifying-session classification (needed for grid position tracking) persists in shared memory long enough after session end in all cases — AMS2_SessionLogger handles SESSION_QUALIFY the same way, suggesting yes, but test in-game
OverTake.gg download pages return HTTP 403 to fetchers, so Simple Endurance Championship Manager details were reconstructed from search snippets rather than the page itself

historical-data.summary:
Research on open/free structured data sources for building historical season packs for an AMS2 career companion app, plus a catalog of era-specific F1 scoring rules a points engine must support.

=== F1: PRIMARY SOURCES ===

1) f1db (github.com/f1db/f1db) — BEST FIT for offline season packs. "Most comprehensive free open source database with all-time Formula 1 racing data", 1950-present, updated after every race (CalVer versioning YYYY.RR.MICRO; latest observed release v2026.8.1, published 2026-06-29, covering 2026 Austrian GP round 8). License: CC BY 4.0 (attribution-only — commercial use OK, ideal for a distributable app). Release assets: f1db-csv.zip, f1db-json-single.zip, f1db-json-splitted.zip, f1db-smile-single.zip, f1db-smile-splitted.zip, f1db-sqlite.zip (~15.5 MB ready-made SQLite DB), f1db-sql-sqlite.zip, f1db-sql-mysql.zip, f1db-sql-postgresql.zip (+ *-single-inserts variants), checksums_sha256.txt. JSON schema at src/schema/current/single/f1db.schema.json (schema v6.4.0). Top-level JSON arrays: drivers, constructors, chassis, engineManufacturers, engines, tyreManufacturers, entrants, circuits, grandsPrix, seasons, races, continents, countries. CRITICAL for entry lists: Season object has year, entrants[], constructors[], engineManufacturers[], tyreManufacturers[], drivers[], driverStandings[], constructorStandings[]. SeasonEntrant = {entrantId, countryId, constructors[]}; SeasonEntrantConstructor = {constructorId, engineManufacturerId, chassis[]:{chassisId}, engines[]:{engineId}, tyreManufacturers[], drivers[]}; SeasonEntrantDriver = {driverId, rounds, roundsText, testDriver} — i.e. full driver-team-chassis-engine-tyre entry lists per season with per-driver round ranges. Race object: id, year, round, date, time, grandPrixId, officialName, circuitId, circuitLayoutId, plus qualifying/practice/sprint results, startingGridPositions, raceResults, fastestLaps, pitStops, driverStandings, constructorStandings. RaceResult fields: positionNumber, positionText, driverId, driverNumber, constructorId, engineManufacturerId, tyreManufacturerId, sharedCar (boolean — directly supports 1950s shared drives!), laps, time, timeMillis, points, gap, gapLaps, reasonRetired, gridPositionNumber, positionsGained, polePosition, fastestLap, driverOfTheDay, grandSlam. Standings objects: positionDisplayOrder, positionNumber, positionText, driverId/constructorId, points, championshipWon. Also ships circuit SVG track maps in src/assets/circuits (black/black-outline/white/white-outline styles). f1db does NOT encode points-system rules — the app must implement scoring itself (see quirks below); f1db's stored per-race points + official standings are ideal for validating the app's points engine against every season 1950-2026.

2) jolpica-f1 (github.com/jolpica/jolpica-f1) — Ergast successor (Ergast shut down start of 2025), drop-in compatible REST API, coverage 1950-present, actively maintained (it is FastF1's backend). Base URL http://api.jolpi.ca/ergast/f1/. Endpoints: /ergast/f1/seasons/, /races/, /results/, /circuits/, /constructors/, /drivers/, /{season}/driverstandings/, /{season}/constructorstandings/, /{season}/qualifying/, /{season}/{round}/laps/, /{season}/{round}/pitstops/, /sprint/, /status/ (end with / or .json; MRData root object). Rate limits: unauthenticated 4 req/s burst, 500 req/hour sustained. License/terms: data CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, non-commercial only without permission (contact admin@jolpi.ca) — MORE RESTRICTIVE than f1db. Bulk CSV database dumps: GET https://api.jolpi.ca/data/dumps/download/ lists dumps (available_types, delay_days, delayed_dumps, latest_dumps with sha256/size/download URL); https://api.jolpi.ca/data/dumps/download/delayed/?dump_type=csv is public (14-day delay); /latest/ requires supporter API key. API docs: https://api.jolpi.ca/docs/#/dumps. NOTE: Ergast/jolpica does NOT provide season entry lists (no driver-team-car entrant tables) — f1db is the only open source with true per-season entrant structures.

3) OpenF1 (openf1.org, base https://api.openf1.org/v1/) — 2023-present ONLY (live timing derived: telemetry, laps, positions, team radio, weather, race control). No pre-2023 history. Free tier 3 req/s / 30 req/min; real-time needs €9.90/mo sponsor tier. License CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, unofficial/educational. Verdict: NOT useful for historical season packs; irrelevant to this app except possibly modern-season packs.

=== OTHER SERIES (no f1db-equivalent exists; all are scrape-or-transcribe HTML stat sites unless noted) ===
- CART/IndyCar/USAC/AAA: ChampCarStats.com — race results, entries, standings, chassis/engine data 1905-2026, HTML tables only, no stated license. Also race-database.com (F1, NASCAR, CART/Champ Car, IRL, Le Mans). Motorsport Stats (results.motorsportstats.com) has deep coverage but is commercial.
- NASCAR: nascaR.data (github.com/kyleGrealis/nascaR.data, GPL-3, data scraped with permission from DriverAverages.com) — Cup 1949-present, Xfinity 1982-present, Trucks 1995-present; direct CSV/Parquet downloads at https://nascar.kylegrealis.com/cup_series.csv, /nxs_series.csv, /truck_series.csv; fields: season, race number, track, finish, start, laps, laps led, points; updated Mondays 5AM EST in season. Also Neil-Paine-1/NASCAR-data (raw CSVs) and unofficial APIs over NASCAR's public CDN (cf.nascar.com feeds, modern era only).
- Group C / World Sportscar Championship: racingsportscars.com (all WSC races, HTML), wsrp.cz "World Sports Racing Prototypes" (season results + entry lists + chassis numbers, HTML; site had TLS cert issues when fetched — use http), Motor Sport Magazine database (motorsportmagazine.com/database).
- DTM: touringcars.net database (DTM complete 1984-2020, HTML), touringcarracing.net (worldwide touring cars 1952-1993, HTML), Autosport TNF forum threads document DTM 1984-1996 points formats.
- F2/F3000: no open structured DB; Wikipedia season articles are the practical primary source (entry lists + results tables are consistently structured wikitext), plus DriverDB.com (50+ championships) and Motorsport Stats (commercial). OldRacingCars.com for chassis-level F2/F5000/Indy data.
- General fallback: Wikipedia/Wikidata — Wikipedia season pages have machine-parseable entry-list and results wikitables for every series above; recommend hand-curated JSON season packs transcribed from these, matching the app's community-extensible data-pack design.

=== ERA-SPECIFIC F1 SCORING QUIRKS THE POINTS ENGINE MUST SUPPORT ===
A) Points tables: 1950-1959: 8-6-4-3-2 + 1 pt fastest lap (FL point drivers-only, split fractionally among drivers tying on FL time — 1954 British GP: 7 drivers got 1/7 pt each); 1960: 8-6-4-3-2-1 (FL point dropped); 1961-1990: 9-6-4-3-2-1; 1991-2002: 10-6-4-3-2-1; 2003-2009: 10-8-6-5-4-3-2-1; 2010-present: 25-18-15-12-10-8-6-4-2-1. 2019-2024: +1 FL point (must finish top 10); abolished 2025. Sprints: 2021: 3-2-1; 2022+: 8-7-6-5-4-3-2-1. 2014: double points at final race (Abu Dhabi) only.
B) Dropped scores / best-N per season (drivers): 1950-53 best 4; 1954-57 best 5; 1958 best 6; 1959 best 5; 1960 best 6; 1961 best 5; 1962 best 5; 1963-65 best 6; 1966 best 5. Split-half seasons 1967-1980 (best N from EACH half separately): 1967: 5 of first 6 + 4 of last 5; 1968: 5 of 6 + 5 of 6; 1969: 5 of 6 + 4 of 5; 1970: 6 of 7 + 5 of 6; 1971: 5 of 6 + 4 of 5; 1972: 5 of 6 + 5 of 6; 1973-74: 7 of 8 + 6 of 7; 1975: 6 of 7 + 6 of 7; 1976: 7 of 8 + 7 of 8; 1977: 8 of 9 + 7 of 8; 1978: 7 of 8 + 7 of 8; 1979: 4 of first 7 + 4 of last 8; 1980: 5 of 7 + 5 of 7. 1981-1990: best 11 of season; 1991+: all results count. (1958-1966 best-N applied to BOTH championships; from 1979 constructors counted all results.)
C) Shared drives: 1950-1957 points split equally among drivers sharing a car regardless of laps driven; from 1958 shared drives score ZERO (e.g. 1958 Italian GP, 1960 Argentine GP). f1db flags these via RaceResult.sharedCar.
D) Half-points races (stopped <75%, ≥2 laps): 1975 Spanish GP, 1975 Austrian GP, 1984 Monaco GP, 1991 Australian GP, 2009 Malaysian GP, 2021 Belgian GP — exactly six. 2022+: replaced by sliding scale: <2 racing laps: 0 pts; 2 laps-25%: 6-4-3-2-1 (top 5); 25-50%: 13-10-8-6-5-4-3-2-1 (top 9); 50-75%: 19-14-12-10-8-6-4-3-2-1 (top 10); ≥75%: full points.
E) Aggregate-time races (result = combined classification of two parts; engine needs a manual/override result-entry mode since an in-game AMS2 race can't reproduce it): 1959 German GP (AVUS — two heats by design, aggregate time/distance), and red-flag restart aggregates: 1978 Austrian, 1978 Italian, 1981 French, 1982 Detroit, 1987 Mexican, 1989 San Marino, 1992 French, 1994 San Marino, 1994 Japanese. Other stoppages counted at point of stoppage (1971 Canadian, 1974 Brazilian, 1975 British, 1990 Portuguese, 1997 Canadian, 2003 Brazilian, 2014 Japanese).
F) Indianapolis 500: championship round 1950-1960 (11 seasons), points to DRIVERS' championship only — never to the constructors' cup (1958-1960); European regulars almost never entered, so per-season driver pools differ per race — the engine must allow race entries outside the season entry list.
G) Constructors: championship (International Cup for F1 Manufacturers) exists only from 1958; 1958-1979 only the HIGHEST-placed car of each constructor scored per race; from 1980 (Wikipedia points-systems article phrases it as 'up until 1979') all cars scored. FL point never counted for constructors in the 1950s.
H) Misc edge cases worth flags in the data model: fractional points (shared drives + shared FL → points like 1/7); drivers excluded from championship while race results stand (1997 Schumacher); count-back tiebreakers on wins/places; period-correct 'points for top 5/6 only' meaning most finishers score nothing.

=== RECOMMENDATION FOR THE APP ===
Use f1db (CC BY 4.0) as the canonical F1 seed: its season entrants (driver-team-chassis-engine-tyre + rounds) map directly onto season-pack entry lists to match against AMS2 custom AI files/skins, and its per-season official standings let you unit-test the points engine against all quirks above. Implement points systems as data-driven JSON rule objects (pointsTable, fastestLapPoint, bestNRules[{fromRound,toRound,count}], sharedDrivePolicy, halfPointsAllowed, constructorsBestCarOnly, indyStyleExclusions) rather than code per era. Use jolpica only as an online lookup/update convenience (mind CC BY-NC-SA), and hand-curated packs from Wikipedia/ChampCarStats/touringcars.net/racingsportscars.com for non-F1 series.

ecosystem.summary: PART A — AMS2 historical F1 skin/custom-AI ecosystem (current as of AMS2 V1.6.9.8, June 2026). Modding stack has two halves. (1) Custom liveries: DDS textures + per-car override XML installed under Documents\Automobilista 2\Vehicles\Textures\CustomLiveries\Overrides\<car folder>; override entries look like <LIVERY_OVERRIDE LIVERY="51" ...>; launch option -showLiveryIDs reveals livery slot IDs; preview images are 2048x600 DXT5 DDS. (2) Custom AI: one XML per vehicle class named exactly after the class (e.g. F-Classic_Gen3.xml, F-V10_Gen1.xml, GT3.xml) placed in <game install>\Automobilista 2\UserData\CustomAIDrivers\ (game folder, NOT Documents). Root element <custom_ai_drivers>, each <driver livery_name="..."> (case-sensitive, must match livery name shown in vehicle-select UI) with child tags: name, country, race_skill, qualifying_skill, aggression, defending, stamina, consistency, start_reactions, wet_skill, tyre_management, fuel_management, blue_flag_conceding, weather_tyre_changes, avoidance_of_mistakes, avoidance_of_forced_mistakes, vehicle_reliability (floats 0.0–1.0, 0.5 = realistic average), plus performance scalars weight_scalar, power_scalar, drag_scalar (0.900–1.100) and setup_downforce / setup_downforce_randomness. The official Reiza thread (forum.reizastudios.com/threads/...21758/) contains the full 150+ class-filename list in a spoiler. Custom AI works only in single player; a custom grid is achieved by setting AI opponent count <= number of overridden drivers. In the built-in Custom Championship (which the app deliberately bypasses) custom AI has known problems: grid capped by the smallest-grid track on the calendar, roster cannot change mid-season, replacement drivers' points get mis-credited, very limited points-system options, no constructors championship, and only 4 championship save slots — these are exactly the gaps a companion app exploits. Typical season pack contents: full-grid liveries for each model in the class (e.g. 1991 pack covers F-Classic Gen4 Model1 V12 / Model2 V10 / Model3 V8 + McLaren MP4/6), Reiza-style preview images, helmets, suits/gloves/visors, a matching CustomAIDrivers XML with real driver names/nationalities/ratings, and increasingly a .bat "XML Selector" (e.g. F1_Season_1991_XML_Selector.bat) that swaps between round-by-round roster scenario XMLs. Packs ship the archive pre-laid-out in "Automobilista 2" folder structure so AMS2CM can one-click install; manual install = copy Vehicles folder to the Documents Overrides path and the XML to CustomAIDrivers. Most popular historical packs (OverTake.gg, mostly by the "IMG" collective with AFry as prolific author): F1 1967 (Formula Vintage Gen1, orig. Damian Baldi), 1969 (F-Vintage Gen2), [F-Retro Gen 1] Formula 1 1975 complete skin pack with AI skills and names (id 41981), Formula Retro Gen 2 1978/79 historic liveries pack (41320), 1983 (F-Retro Gen3), 1985/1986 (F-Classic Gen1), [AMS2] F1 1988 Season (F-Classic Gen2, AFry), [IMG] F1 1990 Season (64765) + F1 1990 custom AI (53488), [AMS2] F1 1991 Season (38880, F-Classic Gen4, ~25.6k downloads, v2.08), [IMG] F1 1992 Season, F1 1993 (F-HiTech Gen1/Gen2), [AMS2] F1 1995 Season (53478, F-V12), F1 1996 Skinpack for Formula V10 Gen 1 (80579), 1997 (F-V10 Gen1), 2000 (F-V10 Gen2), 2012 (Formula Reiza), 2021 (F-Ultimate Gen1), 2022+ (F-Ultimate Gen2). V1.6.9.8 added new classes for 2005/2006/2008 GP seasons (F-V10 Gen3, F-V8 Gen1) with Bridgestone-vs-Michelin runners, so packs for those years are emerging. Ecosystem keystones: "NAMeS: Real Drivers for AMS2" (50989; replaces 2,800+ fictional grid slots with real drivers across ALL classes incl. DLC — important because multiclass races require custom-AI files for every class present or the grid won't populate), NAMeS+AI for mods (60634), AMS2 Custom AI Performance by DannyDB (79113), and the OverTake "Ultimate F1 Season Guide" (news id 567, fully revamped for 2026) which maps every season to a class+pack. The AMS2 Skinning Project Discord is the upstream repository for many packs. PART B — COMPETITORS. (1) Rewind GP by MazzFrascuorno (OverTake 82303, new ~late 2025/2026) — the closest competitor: historical F1 career mode using custom liveries+AI profiles, multi-season alternate-timeline careers, reputation system + driver market (you can lose your seat), automatic result capture via AMS2 shared memory, per-save history of every drivers/constructors championship; extensible via downloadable season packs (1994 id 82790, 1996, 1997) and a "Season Pack Creator for RewindGP" (83144). Gaps: F1-only, few seasons so far, no team-finance/tycoon layer, depends on shared-memory capture. (2) Race Pace: A Living Career App (81848; OverTake news 4562, first release Jan 2026, 14 updates through May 2026) — persistent multi-discipline world, teams with principals/expectations, earn seats, promotion/demotion; separate-window companion. Modern/generic, not historical-era focused, no era points systems. (3) For the Win – Career Mode for AMS 2 (63157, Marcos Santos, free exe + datafiles folder, v1.2.2, last update 2024-07-12, stalled) — 10 career paths (Kart→F1, IndyCar, NASCAR, etc.), license C→S via sponsor objectives, special events (Le Mans 24h, Indy 500, Daytona 500, Bathurst 1000). (4) Racing Life (55558) — richest tycoon features (money, bank loans, fame, sponsor contracts, buy/lease cars) but discontinued; successor "Racing Life World". (5) abesto/ams2-career (GitHub, Unlicense; hosted at ams2-career.abesto.net) — React/TypeScript/Redux web app, manual race logging, XP/grade progression, static CSV data in src/app/data/ synced from a Google Sheet via `yarn run update-data`; simple, no teams/economy. (6) Second Monitor (Winzarten, GitLab, .NET 8, v9.39.0) — timing/telemetry app with SP Championships (real-series calendar templates incl. historic F1, auto result capture from telemetry, F1/IMSA scoring options, multiclass, team championships) and an AI-difficulty Rating module; no career/economy/narrative. (7) AMS2 Custom Drivers Utility (FitzHastings/AMS2CustomDriversUtil, Java 8, Apache-2.0; OverTake 61336) — GUI editor/generator for custom AI XML grids: sliders for every parameter, driver libraries, livery dropdown per class, "Championship Table Grid Generator" that spaces AI skill proportionally to real points tables. (8) AMS2CM (OpenSimTools/AMS2CM, GitHub) — one-click installer for AMS2 mods incl. skinpacks/custom AI. (9) cauld/race_mgr — self-hosted web UI for AMS2 dedicated servers, ONLINE championships with history. (10) ifeelgitty/Racing-Career-Manager — generic cross-sim career sim driven by CSV car/series tables. (11) Built-in Custom Championship editor — limitations listed above. Market context: OverTake reports Reiza is targeting an OFFICIAL AMS2 career mode toward end of 2026. Open gaps a career-tycoon app can fill: era-correct points/rules engines (dropped scores, best-N results, shared drives), constructors + drivers standings, team finances/bankruptcy/aging/retirement (nothing active does economy), round-by-round entry lists (the XML-Selector pattern proves demand), manual result entry as a first-class deliberate flow (Rewind GP/Second Monitor need shared memory; For the Win/abesto are manual but shallow), a season-pack format that references the existing OverTake skinpack+CustomAIDrivers XML ecosystem instead of shipping its own content, coverage of all eras 1967→2008+ across Formula Vintage→F-V8 Gen1 classes, and single-exe lightweight distribution.
