career-design.proposal:
# Career / Tycoon Layer — Design Proposal
*(AMS2 Historical Career Companion — "the app runs the world, AMS2 runs the race")*

## 0. Design pillars

1. **The sim never decides on-track outcomes.** All racing happens in AMS2 single races; the career layer only *shapes inputs* (generated Custom AI XML: skills, form, reliability, performance scalars) and *consumes outputs* (imported results). Nothing in the economy is resolved by a hidden dice-roll race sim — this is the core honesty contract with the player and the thing no competitor (Rewind GP, Race Pace, For the Win) gets fully right.
2. **Deterministic, journaled, replayable.** One master seed per save. Every random draw comes from a named, derived stream; every state change is an append-only journal entry with a cause. Given the same save file and the same imported results, the world evolves identically.
3. **Canon by default, divergence by choice.** Historical season packs are the world's ground truth. The sim perturbs canon only where the player's presence forces it, unless the player opts into a "living world" that can drift into an alternate timeline.
4. **Everything is data.** Ratings curves, points rules, salary bands, era inflation, event decks — all JSON in season/era packs, community-extensible.

---

## 1. Player roles

### Role A — Driver for hire (MVP)
The player is a driver with ratings-facing *market attributes* (reputation, overperformance record, age, nationality) but whose actual pace is their real driving. They sign contracts, earn salary and prize percentages, get offers, get fired, retire. Team economies run in the background and are visible but not controllable.

### Role B — Owner-driver (Later, Phase 3)
The player owns (or founds/buys) a team: sets budget allocation (car development, reliability, second driver, staff), negotiates sponsors, and also drives. Failure mode is bankruptcy — career over, or humbling fallback to Role A if another team will still have you. Role B reuses every Role A system plus the team ledger controls; it is strictly additive, which is why it phases cleanly.

A third flavor — **privateer** (buy a customer car, enter selected rounds; very period-correct for 1967–1975) — falls out of Role B mechanics almost free and is scheduled with it.

---

## 2. The career loop

```
PRE-SEASON            PER ROUND (×N)                          POST-SEASON
┌────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐   ┌───────────────┐
│ contracts   │   │ 1 pre-race news + form update        │   │ standings via │
│ budgets     │──▶│ 2 generate CustomAIDrivers XML       │──▶│ era rules eng │
│ car ratings │   │ 3 player races in AMS2 (Q + R)       │   │ prize money   │
│ expectations│   │ 4 import result (shared mem/SM/manual)│  │ season review │
└────────────┘   │ 5 apply: points, money, rep, wear,    │   │ driver market │
                 │   morale, mid-season events           │   │ aging/retire  │
                 └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │ team dev      │
                                                            │ era advance?  │
                                                            └───────────────┘
```

Each numbered step is a pure function `(state, inputs, seedStream) → (state', journalEntries[])`. Step 4 is the only external input.

---

## 3. Deterministic sim core

- **PRNG**: PCG32. `seed = H(masterSeed, subsystem, year, round, entityId)` — e.g. `H(seed,"form",1974,3,"driver.ronnie_peterson")`. No shared global RNG; re-running any phase from a snapshot reproduces byte-identical output. Importing the same result twice is idempotent (results carry a hash; the journal rejects duplicates).
- **Journal**: append-only list of `{seq, phase, entity, field, delta, cause, seedStream}`. The UI's "why?" affordance on any number (rep, cash, rating) walks the journal. This is the debuggability requirement made concrete, and it doubles as the news-feed source.
- **Save format**: one folder per career: `career.json` (snapshot), `journal.ndjson`, `results/` (raw imported result files, kept verbatim so the sim can be fully re-derived after a bug fix — "re-simulate from round 1" is a menu item).
- **Robustness to weird results**: the sim must accept anything AMS2 produces (player quits mid-race → treated as DNF-withdrew; restarted race → player chooses which import counts; sandbagging → the overperformance model just sees bad results). No result is ever "invalid" to the economy.

---

## 4. Ratings model (the world's hidden state)

All entities use 0–100 internal ratings; XML floats are derived at generation time.

**Driver (AI)**: `pace`, `qualiPace`, `consistency`, `aggression`, `wetSkill`, `experience`, plus dynamic `form` (rolling ±8, updated from last 3–5 imported results vs expectation) and optional per-track affinities (street/power/high-speed tags). Historical drivers ship with pack-authored ratings; the app can also import ratings from existing community custom-AI XMLs (reverse-map floats → 0–100) so the huge OverTake pack ecosystem seeds the world.

**Team**: `carRating` (chassis+engine pace), `reliabilityRating`, `developmentRate`, `prestige`, `budgetTier` (1–5 MVP; full ledger later), `expectation` (target WCC position, derived from budget rank ± prestige).

**Player**: no pace rating — instead a **pace anchor** (see §9) plus the same market attributes as AI drivers: `reputation`, `age`, `overperformanceIndex`.

---

## 5. Economy

### 5.1 Currency and eras
All money is in **Budget Units (BU)**, normalized so the era's top team budget ≈ 100 BU/season. Era packs supply a display skin (£ 1967, $ 1985…) and an inflation index; on era transition, balances rescale automatically. This sidesteps modeling five decades of real currency while keeping period flavor.

### 5.2 Driver income (MVP)
- **Salary**: from contract; seat salary bands scale with team budget tier (tier-1 seat ≈ 3–8 BU, backmarker ≈ 0.2–0.8 BU, era-modified: 1967 drivers earn mostly from start/prize money — bands are pack data).
- **Prize/start money**: per-round table in the season pack (era-correct: 1960s = start money + prize per finishing position; modern = nothing to the driver directly, folded into salary).
- **Personal sponsors** (Phase 2): 1–2 slots unlocked by reputation; pay per season + win bonuses; carry scandal risk.
Driver money is deliberately light-touch in Role A: it's a scorecard, a retirement "career earnings" stat, and — later — the capital requirement for buying into team ownership.

### 5.3 Team economy (full model, phased)
**MVP (tier model)**: teams have `budgetTier` 1–5 driving car dev, salaries, and AI scalars. Tiers drift ±1 between seasons from a deterministic function of (WCC finish vs expectation, prestige, event modifiers). Insolvency is event-driven only (canon folds from pack data, or a "financial crisis" event on a tier-5 team that badly missed expectations two years running).

**Phase 2 (ledger model)**: per-season team ledger:
- *Income*: title/secondary sponsorship `S = base(prestige) × health(sponsor) + bonus(results)`, prize fund from previous WCC position (pack-defined pot), manufacturer backing (event-driven), owner wealth injection (pack flag: e.g. patron teams).
- *Costs*: entry fees, chassis build + crash repairs (from result: player DNF cause prompt / AI DNF `FinishStatus`; a wrecked car costs 1–3 BU and can force an older-spec entry = temporary scalar penalty), engines (supplier contract: works/customer — customer engines cost BU and cap `carRating`), staff wage bill (scales with dev rate), car development spend.
- Cash < 0 → **crisis ladder** (§7).

---

## 6. Contracts, offers, and the driver market

### 6.1 Expectations
Every seat carries an expectation derived from team state: `expectedFinish(driver) = rank of (teamCarRating + driverPaceEstimate)` across the generated entry list. For the player, `driverPaceEstimate` = pace anchor. This same computation prices the **overperformance index (OPI)**:

`OPI_race = expectedFinish − actualFinish` (positive = overachieved), smoothed: `OPI = 0.8·OPI + 0.2·OPI_race`, DNFs attributed by cause (mechanical DNF ≈ neutral, crash ≈ −2).

OPI is the engine of the whole market: winning in a Ferrari is expected; dragging a tier-4 car to P6 is a signing beacon. It also makes sandbagging self-correcting — bad results lower your expected finish next time.

### 6.2 Offer generation (deterministic)
At season end (and mid-season when a seat opens via event/injury/firing):
1. Each team with an open seat computes a shortlist score for every available driver: `score = w₁·reputation + w₂·OPI + w₃·experience − w₄·salaryDemand − w₅·ageRisk` (weights per team archetype: top teams weight reputation, ambitious midfielders weight OPI, backmarkers weight pay-driver money — yes, **pay-driver seats** are a first-class, era-correct mechanic: low rep + personal sponsor cash can buy a 1990 Coloni seat).
2. Teams offer down their shortlist; drivers accept by mirrored preference (car rating, salary, prestige, loyalty). Player offers are surfaced as 0–3 cards with salary, length (1–2 yr), expectation clause ("score points 4 times"), and a **firing clause** (miss expectation badly for ~40% of a season → mid-season sack risk; the sim warns via news first).
3. All tie-breaks by seeded stream `market/year`. The displaced-driver chain resolves deterministically: whoever the player replaces enters the market and may take the player's old seat (MVP: simple seat-swap; Phase 2: full multi-round market resolution).

### 6.3 Canon dial
- **Canonical (MVP default)**: rosters follow pack entry lists each season; the market only resolves the player's seat + its displacement chain.
- **Living world (Phase 2)**: pack rosters are only the initial condition; the market, aging, and finances evolve rosters freely → alternate timelines ("what if Amon signs for Lotus in '70").

---

## 7. Team financial health and bankruptcy spirals

The spiral (Phase 2, ledger model): results below expectation → sponsor `health` decays → next-season income drops → dev spend cut → `carRating` falls → worse results. Deliberately positive-feedback, but **damped and legible**:
- Income floors (patron minimums) pace a collapse over ~1.5–2 seasons, never one bad race.
- **Crisis ladder** (deterministic thresholds on cash): (1) news warning + hiring freeze → (2) sell a driver / take pay-driver → (3) skip flyaway rounds (entry removed from generated XML — grids shrink like real history) → (4) fold or rescue. Rescue draws (buyout, new patron, merger) come from the event deck with probability weighted by prestige — famous names get saved more often, true to life.
- Folding removes the lineage from future rounds and pays out the journal; if it's the *player's* team, contract void → emergency market pass (mid-season offers weighted down — realistic scramble).
- Runaway dominance is damped from the other side: diminishing returns on dev spend above 100 BU and era rule-change events that partially reset `carRating` (ground effect ban, turbo ban — authored in era packs).

---

## 8. Aging, decline, retirement

**AI drivers**: age-based multiplier curve on `pace`/`consistency` (peak ~26–33, era-shifted: 1950s–60s curves peak later and stretch — pack parameter). `experience` rises to offset early decline, so late-career drivers get slower but steadier — visible in generated XML (`race_skill` ↓, `consistency` ↑). Retirement probability from (age, era table, recent OPI, seat quality); announcements are pre-drawn at season start (seeded) so news can foreshadow ("Brabham hints this year may be his last"). Replacements: pack-listed historical rookies first (canon), generated rookies (name/nation tables per era) in living world.

**Player**: the app cannot slow the player's hands, so aging acts through the market (age enters offer scoring; 40-year-olds get one-year deals from midfielders, not Ferrari) and through an optional **Hardcore Aging** toggle that applies a small growing `weight_scalar`/`power_scalar` handicap to the player's livery after the decline age (the v1.6.9.8 scalars affect the player's car — the one legitimate hook we have). Retirement is the player's choice; a retirement ceremony screen summarizes the journal into a career record (titles, wins, earnings, era span). Retired careers can spawn a Role-B second act ("buy a team") in Phase 3.

**Injuries**: off by default (toggle). When on: injuries only from *events* (testing crash, road accident — era-weighted) or from player-reported race crashes; effect = miss N rounds (replacement driver seated by market logic) + temporary consistency malus. Never career-ending unless the player enables "brutal era realism" (pre-1980 mortality is handled abstractly as career-ending injury only, and only for AI — taste guardrail).

---

## 9. Feeding the tycoon back into AMS2 (the killer feature)

Before every round the app writes `<class>.xml` to `UserData\CustomAIDrivers\` (with timestamped backup/restore of anything it didn't write):

1. **Effective skill**: `eff = basePace + form + trackAffinity + morale`; normalize across the round's entry list into `race_skill`/`qualifying_skill` 0.0–1.0 (using the full range, since the game compresses around the difficulty slider).
2. **Car**: `power_scalar`/`weight_scalar`/`drag_scalar` from `carRating` mapped through the era's authored spread (1967 spread ≫ 1991 spread), plus mid-season upgrade step-changes when a team's dev lands ("Lotus brings the 72 update at round 5").
3. **Reliability**: `vehicle_reliability` from `reliabilityRating` (+ engine supplier modifier). AI attrition then *actually happens in-game* and comes back in the result import — the app never fakes a DNF.
4. **Per-track overrides** (`tracks=` attr) for specialists and one-off form stories.
5. **Consistency/aggression/wet** straight from ratings; `setup_downforce` bias for era flavor.

So "a rich rival's AI gets faster next season" is literally: bigger budget → dev points → higher `carRating` → higher scalars and skills in next season's generated XML. The player *feels* the economy on track.

**Player pace anchor & difficulty calibration**: after each import, compare the player's qualifying gap and race pace to AI entries whose exact generated values are known. Update `anchor` by exponential smoothing (pure function of results — deterministic). The anchor (a) recommends the AMS2 Opponent Skill slider per class ("run 102% for a realistic midfield fight"), (b) sets the player's expected finish for OPI, and (c) lets the app re-center AI skill normalization so the championship battle stays honest as the player improves. First-run: a one-race calibration event ("pre-season test") seeds the anchor.

**Player car**: the player's livery gets scalars too — your tier-4 seat really is slower than the Ferrari. This is the tycoon made physical, and it's why contract decisions matter.

---

## 10. Events and news

A **season event deck**: authored templates in era packs, instantiated deterministically at season start (seeded draws, scheduled to rounds) plus reactive triggers from state (crisis ladder, expectation misses, title deciders). Categories: sponsor scandal/withdrawal, new patron/manufacturer entry or exit, engine supplier changes, rule-change announcements (foreshadowing next era pack), driver injury (if enabled), silly-season rumors (leaking real pending market computations — the rumor system reads the actual shortlists, so rumors are *true-ish*), milestone/flavor items from the journal (first win, 100th start). Every event is a journal entry with parameters + a text template → the news feed is generated, never hand-waved, and fully reproducible. Canon mode weights the deck toward what really happened (packs can script canon events: "Honda withdraws end of 1968").

---

## 11. Era transitions (1967 → 1980s → …)

- The career clock is a **year timeline**; season packs declare `{year, class, calendar, entryList, rules, economyParams}`. Advancing past the last year of an era hands over to the next installed pack.
- **Lineage IDs** (`team.lotus`, `driver.g_hill`, `engine.cosworth`) are the carryover keys: prestige, finances (rescaled by inflation index), player relationship history, and rivalries follow lineage across packs. Teams absent from the next pack fold/merge per pack-authored mapping (or a deterministic default: fold with farewell news).
- Ratings don't carry raw across eras; they re-derive from the new pack's authored values blended with carried prestige/OPI (regression to era mean) — so a living-world juggernaut stays strong but the new era is a genuine reset opportunity.
- The player just… keeps driving: new class, new calendar, points engine switches rule set, news covers the transition ("3-litre formula announced for 1966"). A career from F-Vintage Gen1 to F-Hybrid Gen3 is ~25+ packs — the pack format is the product.
- Missing years between installed packs: bridge mode (skip with a summarized interregnum) or block, player's choice.

## 12. MVP cut rationale
MVP = Role A on canon rosters with the tier economy: this ships a complete, emotionally whole career (drive, overperform, get the Ferrari offer, age, retire) while deferring the systems that need balancing time (ledger economy, living market, owner mode). Every Phase 2/3 system is designed above so MVP data structures (journal, lineage, seeds, OPI) already support them — no schema rewrites later.
mvp: Role A driver-for-hire career: contracts, salary, seat expectations, firing risk, player-chosen retirement
Deterministic sim core: master seed, named PCG32 seed streams per subsystem/year/round/entity, append-only journal with cause tracking, 'why?' inspector on any number
Save format: career.json snapshot + journal + verbatim raw result files, with idempotent result import and full re-simulation from round 1
Reputation system + overperformance index (OPI) computed from expected-vs-actual finish using the app's own generated AI values
End-of-season offer generation: 0-3 contract cards from expectation/OPI/reputation scoring with team archetype weights, pay-driver seats, deterministic tie-breaks, simple displaced-driver seat swap
Driver-side economy: salary, era-correct start/prize money tables from season packs, Budget Units currency with era display skins, career earnings ledger
Team budget tiers (1-5) with deterministic between-season drift from results-vs-expectation; tiers drive car ratings, salary bands, and generated AI strength

Per-round CustomAIDrivers XML generation: skills from base+form+track affinity normalized across entry list, power/weight/drag scalars from team car rating with per-era spread, vehicle_reliability from team rating, per-track overrides, backup/restore of existing files
Player pace anchor: post-race calibration from known AI values, difficulty slider recommendation, expected-finish placement for OPI, one-race pre-season calibration event
Player livery scalars reflecting the contracted team's car rating (the economy made physical)
Canon roster mode: entry lists follow historical season packs each year; sim resolves only the player's seat and its displacement
AI driver aging curves (era-shifted peaks), canon retirements from pack data with seeded pre-season foreshadowing, pack-listed historical rookie replacements
Dynamic per-round form (rolling last-3-5 results vs expectation) feeding XML deltas
News feed generated from journal entries + small template event deck (retirements, signings, milestones, canon-scripted events like team folds)
Era transition v1: year timeline, lineage IDs for teams/drivers/engines, prestige and money carryover with inflation rescale, rule-set switch for points engine, bridge-or-block for missing packs
Mechanical-vs-crash DNF attribution at import (from FinishStatus plus one-tap player prompt) feeding OPI and news
later: Phase 2: full team ledger economy — sponsorship income with health decay, prize fund by WCC position, engine supplier contracts (works vs customer), entry fees, crash repair costs, staff wage bill, development spend with diminishing returns

Phase 2: bankruptcy spirals with damped feedback — crisis ladder (warning, driver sale/pay-driver, skipped rounds removing entries from generated XML, fold-or-rescue), prestige-weighted rescue/buyout events, player-team collapse triggering emergency market pass
Phase 2: living-world mode — full multi-round driver market resolution, evolving rosters, generated rookies from era name/nation tables, alternate-timeline divergence from canon
Phase 2: personal sponsor slots for the driver with win bonuses and scandal risk; sponsor scandal/withdrawal events
Phase 2: mid-season driver market — injury/firing replacements, silly-season rumor system that leaks true pending shortlist computations
Phase 2: mid-season car development step-changes (upgrade lands at round N, scalars shift) and rule-change events partially resetting car ratings between eras

Phase 3: owner-driver mode — budget allocation (development, reliability, second driver, staff), sponsor negotiation, second-seat hiring, bankruptcy as career fail state with fallback to driver-for-hire
Phase 3: privateer mode — buy a customer car, enter selected rounds, start-money economics (1967-1975 flavor)
Injury system (opt-in toggle): event-driven and crash-reported injuries, missed rounds with replacement drivers, temporary consistency malus, optional brutal-era realism for AI only
Hardcore Aging toggle: growing weight/power scalar handicap on the player's livery past decline age
Rivalries, morale, media interactions and personality traits influencing offers and events
Multi-series careers: guest drives (Indy 500, Le Mans) using non-F1 classes, cross-discipline reputation
Second-act careers: retired player buys a team; dynasty/heir mode
Sim inspector / replay-debug console exposing seed streams and journal diffs for pack authors and balancing
Community balancing hooks: era packs overriding aging curves, salary bands, event decks, scalar spreads
risks: Reiza's official AMS2 career mode (targeted end of 2026) could land mid-development; differentiation must stay on historical eras, era-correct rules, and the tycoon economy — things Reiza is unlikely to ship

Performance scalars (v1.6.9.8, ~0.900-1.100) are the only lever on the player's car: the spread may be too small to represent 1967-grade car gaps, and applying handicaps to the player's livery may feel opaque or unfair if not clearly surfaced in UI
race_skill compression around the Opponent Skill slider limits AI spread; if the player sets the slider contrary to app recommendations, expected-finish and OPI computations degrade

Pace-anchor calibration can be gamed (sandbagging) or destabilized by wild results (player quits, restarts, wrong car); needs damping and manual override, and mis-calibration poisons the contract market
Balancing coupled feedback loops (bankruptcy spiral, dominance snowball) is genuinely hard; without floors/damping tested across 20+ season simulations, living-world mode produces absurd timelines

Authoring burden: every era needs pack data (entry lists, ratings, salary bands, event decks, lineage maps); MVP quality depends on shipping 2-3 exemplar packs and a validator, or community adoption stalls
Dependence on the community skin/custom-AI ecosystem: livery_name matching is case-sensitive and fragile; a pack update or game update renaming classes (as v1.6.9 did) silently breaks generated XML
AMS2 shared-memory struct or CustomAIDrivers schema may change in 1.7+; result import and XML generation need version guards and a manual-entry fallback path always available
Determinism vs. floating point and re-simulation: journal replay after app updates must tolerate rule changes (versioned sim functions) or 'resimulate from round 1' breaks old saves
Player trust/cheating: users can hand-edit generated XML or fake results; the design tolerates it (single-player), but leaderboard/sharing features would need integrity checks
Scope creep: the full ledger economy plus living market plus owner mode is a Football-Manager-sized sim; the phased cut must be enforced or the single-exe lightweight promise dies
Sensitive-content taste risk around historical driver mortality/injury eras; abstracted injury handling and toggles mitigate but need care in news text generation

data-architecture.proposal:
# AMS2 Historical Career Companion — Data Architecture Proposal

## 1. Stack: .NET 10 LTS + WPF, published as a single self-contained exe

| Criterion | WPF (.NET 10) | Avalonia | Tauri | Electron |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Toolchain on a machine with **no Node/Python** | One installer: .NET SDK | One installer: .NET SDK | Rust **+** Node/npm + WebView2 tooling | Node/npm mandatory |
| Single-exe distribution | `PublishSingleFile` self-contained, ~70–90 MB, zero prerequisites | Same mechanism, similar size | Small exe but MSI/WebView2 dance | 150–250 MB unpacked; fails the "lightweight" brief |
| Data-heavy UI (grids, standings tables) | Best-in-class (DataGrid, virtualization, 20 yrs of maturity) | Good, younger | HTML tables (fine) but all logic crosses an IPC bridge | Good but heavy |
| Shared-memory (`$pcars2$`) + FileSystemWatcher + XML gen + SQLite | All first-party BCL; **CrewChiefV4's C# struct marshalling can be ported directly** | Same (also .NET) | Rewrite marshalling in Rust; lose the reference implementation | Native addon or ffi pain |
| Windows-only target (per brief) | Perfect fit | Pays a cross-platform tax for nothing today | — | — |

**Recommendation: WPF on .NET 10 LTS** (supported to Nov 2028; .NET 8 LTS support ends Nov 2026, so don't start there). Rationale in one line: every hard integration in this app — memory-mapped file reads of the PC2 struct, watching Second Monitor's Reports folder, generating UTF-8 XML, SQLite — is first-party .NET, and the single working reference codebase for AMS2 shared-memory marshalling (CrewChiefV4) is C#. The dev machine needs exactly one install (the .NET SDK; VS Community optional). Avalonia is the designated fallback if cross-platform ever matters — the architecture below keeps all logic out of the UI layer specifically so a UI swap is cheap.

**Solution layout (enforces testability):**
- `Companion.Core` — domain model, points engine, pack loader/validator. *No I/O, no WPF, no DB references.*
- `Companion.Data` — SQLite persistence (Microsoft.Data.Sqlite), pack file I/O, migrations.
- `Companion.Ams2` — Steam install detection, custom-AI XML writer, `$pcars2$` reader, Second Monitor watcher, bundled class/livery/track library.
- `Companion.App` — WPF shell (MVVM, CommunityToolkit.Mvvm).
- `Companion.Tests` — xunit; includes the f1db oracle suite (§4).

## 2. Storage: SQLite for career state, JSON for season packs

**Career state → one SQLite file per career** (e.g. `MyCareer.ams2career`, WAL mode). Careers are long-lived, relational (drivers × teams × seasons × rounds × results × finances × contracts), constantly re-queried for standings/history screens, and must survive crashes mid-write — that's a database, not a JSON blob. JSON saves were rejected because: whole-file rewrite risks corruption on crash, history queries ("all podiums at Monza across 5 seasons") become O(everything) in-memory scans, and schema migration of nested JSON is far more painful than versioned SQL migrations (a `schema_version` table + sequential migration scripts).

Key tables: `career`, `season`, `round`, `entry`, `driver_state` (age, reputation, form), `team_state` (finances, prestige), `contract`, `race_result_raw` (**the untouched capture JSON as a blob** — lets us re-run scoring after engine bug fixes), `classification`, `standings_cache`, `event_log` (the career narrative feed).

**Season data packs → plain JSON folders**, because community authors must be able to write them in Notepad and share them as zips:
```
%USERPROFILE%\Documents\AMS2CareerCompanion\
  Packs\<packId>\pack.json + season.json + (optional) rounds\*.json
  Careers\*.ams2career
```
Two critical policies:
1. **Packs are immutable inputs.** The app validates on import (JSON Schema shipped in the exe) and never writes to them.
2. **Careers pin their pack.** When a season starts, the resolved pack JSON is copied into the career DB (plus a content hash). A pack update on disk can never corrupt a running season; the player is offered a migration diff instead.

## 3. Season data pack schema (v1)

The pack deliberately **references** the existing ecosystem (OverTake skin packs + CustomAIDrivers conventions) rather than shipping content. All AMS2 bindings use the game's internal `xml_name` identifiers, validated against a bundled, independently updatable snapshot of the AMS2CustomDriversUtil vehicle/track libraries.

**`pack.json` (manifest):**
```json
{
  "schemaVersion": "1.0",
  "packId": "img.f1.1988",
  "name": "Formula One 1988",
  "version": "1.2.0",
  "authors": ["..."],
  "license": "CC-BY-4.0",
  "gameVersionTested": "1.6.9.8",
  "requires": {
    "dlc": ["Brazilian Racing Legends"],
    "skinPacks": [{ "name": "[AMS2] F1 1988 Season", "url": "https://overtake.gg/downloads/...", "required": true }]
  },
  "series": { "seriesId": "f1", "name": "FIA Formula One World Championship", "tier": 1 }
}
```

**`season.json` — calendar with AMS2 track binding:**
```json
{
  "year": 1988,
  "vehicleClasses": ["F-Classic_Gen2"],
  "calendar": [{
    "round": 1,
    "name": "Brazilian Grand Prix",
    "date": "1988-04-03",
    "championship": true,
    "track": {
      "displayName": "Jacarepaguá",
      "ams2Track": "Jacarepagua_Historic_1988",
      "fallbackTracks": ["Jacarepagua_2005"],
      "laps": 60,
      "raceDistanceKm": 302
    },
    "session": { "startTime": "13:00", "weather": ["Clear"] },
    "scoring": { "multiplier": 1.0, "halfPoints": false },
    "allowGuestEntries": false,
    "guestEntries": [],
    "aiRoundOverrides": [{ "entryId": "lotus-1", "race_skill": 0.90 }]
  }]
}
```
`championship: false` models non-championship races; `allowGuestEntries` + `guestEntries` model Indy-500-style rounds whose entrants sit outside the season entry list; `fallbackTracks` handles calendars where AMS2 lacks the period venue (Zandvoort, Mexico…).

**Entry list — teams / drivers / entries (car–seat–rounds):**
```json
"teams": [{
  "teamId": "mclaren", "name": "Honda Marlboro McLaren", "engine": "Honda V6t", "country": "GBR",
  "ams2": {
    "vehicleClass": "F-Classic_Gen2",
    "carModel": "McLaren MP4/4",
    "performance": { "power_scalar": 1.030, "drag_scalar": 0.985, "weight_scalar": 1.000 },
    "vehicle_reliability": 0.85,
    "setup_downforce": 0.6
  },
  "tycoon": { "prestige": 0.95, "budgetTier": 5 }
}],
"drivers": [{
  "driverId": "ayrton-senna", "name": "Ayrton Senna", "country": "BRA", "born": "1960-03-21",
  "ratings": { "race_skill": 0.98, "qualifying_skill": 1.00, "aggression": 0.85, "defending": 0.95,
               "stamina": 0.95, "consistency": 0.90, "start_reactions": 0.95, "wet_skill": 1.00,
               "tyre_management": 0.85, "avoidance_of_mistakes": 0.85, "avoidance_of_forced_mistakes": 0.85 },
  "trackForm": [{ "ams2Tracks": ["Azure_Circuit_2021"], "race_skill": 1.00 }]
}],
"entries": [{
  "entryId": "mclaren-12", "teamId": "mclaren", "driverId": "ayrton-senna",
  "number": 12, "rounds": "1-16",
  "ams2LiveryName": "McLaren MP4/4 #12 Ayrton Senna"
}]
```
`ams2LiveryName` is the **exact, case-sensitive livery display name** from the skin pack's `LIVERY_OVERRIDE NAME` attribute — the sole binding mechanism to custom AI. `rounds` ranges give round-by-round rosters (mid-season driver swaps) without scenario-file hacks. `performance` scalars encode period-correct car spread (and apply to the player's car too — a feature: the player inherits their team's machinery).

**Points system — data, not code:**
```json
"pointsSystem": {
  "name": "FIA 1988",
  "pointsTable": { "1": 9, "2": 6, "3": 4, "4": 3, "5": 2, "6": 1 },
  "fastestLap": { "points": 0, "mustFinishTopN": null, "splitOnTie": false },
  "bestN": { "mode": "splitSeason", "segments": [{ "rounds": "1-8", "count": 7 }, { "rounds": "9-16", "count": 7 }] },
  "sharedDrivePolicy": "zero",
  "halfPointsRule": "legacy75",
  "constructors": { "exists": true, "bestCarOnly": false, "excludedRounds": [] },
  "tiebreak": ["points", "countbackWins", "countbackPlaces"],
  "allowFractionalPoints": false
}
```
This vocabulary covers every documented F1 era quirk: 1950s FL point with fractional ties (`splitOnTie` + fractional arithmetic), best-N whole-season and split-season variants (1950–1990), shared drives (`split` pre-1958 / `zero` after), the six legacy half-points races (per-round `halfPoints` flag) and the 2022+ sliding scale (`halfPointsRule: "slidingScale2022"`), constructors best-car-only 1958–1979, constructors-excluded rounds (Indy 500), and countback tiebreakers. Seed F1 packs are generated from **f1db (CC BY 4.0)** — its `SeasonEntrant → constructor → chassis/engine → drivers(rounds)` structures map 1:1 onto this entry list.

## 4. Standings/points engine: a pure, oracle-tested module

`Companion.Core.Scoring` is a pure function library — no I/O, no clock, no DB:

- **Input:** `SeasonRules` (deserialized `pointsSystem` + per-round scoring flags) and an ordered list of `RaceClassification` records (round, entryId/driverId/teamId, finish position, laps, `FinishStatus`, `sharedWith[]`, fastest-lap flag, race-distance-completed %).
- **Output:** immutable `SeasonStandings` — per-round snapshots of driver and constructor tables, each row carrying *gross* points, *counted* points, and the explicit list of dropped results (so the UI can show "(12) 9th-place dropped" like period yearbooks).
- Points are computed as exact rationals (numerator/denominator) so 1954-British-GP-style 1/7-point splits round-trip losslessly; display formatting is the UI's problem.
- Guest entries score race results without joining the championship; `excluded` drivers (1997-Schumacher rule) keep race results but vanish from standings.

**Test oracle:** a build-time test suite loads f1db's per-season official `driverStandings`/`constructorStandings` for 1950–2026, replays each season's raw results through the engine, and asserts equality. Any era rule we've mis-modelled fails CI immediately. This is the single highest-leverage quality investment in the project and it's nearly free because f1db ships the answers.

## 5. Custom-AI file generation pipeline

Correction to the brief: AMS2 custom AI files are **XML, not JSON**, and live in the **game install** directory: `<Steam>\steamapps\common\Automobilista 2\UserData\CustomAIDrivers\<VehicleClass>.xml` (e.g. `F-Classic_Gen2.xml`).

Pre-race flow ("Race Day" screen):
1. **Locate install** via Steam registry + `libraryfolders.vdf` parse (manual folder picker fallback); create `CustomAIDrivers\` if missing.
2. **Resolve the round's grid**: entries active for this round (rounds ranges + career-state driver swaps + guest entries), merged ratings (driver baseline → `trackForm` → round overrides → career-sim form modifiers later).
3. **Preflight validation**: class filename checked against the bundled vehicle library; every `ams2LiveryName` checked against (a) the bundled stock-livery library and (b) a scan of the user's installed `CustomLiveries\Overrides\*.xml` `NAME` attributes — mismatch = red warning *before* the race, killing the #1 failure mode (case/special-char livery mismatches). Grid size vs. track AI cap warned here too.
4. **Backup, then write**: any existing class XML (e.g. the user's NAMeS file) is timestamped into a backup store with one-click restore. Emit UTF-8, XML-escaped `<custom_ai_drivers>` with `<driver livery_name="...">` entries — exact field names per the verified schema (`race_skill` … `vehicle_reliability`, plus `power_scalar`/`drag_scalar`/`weight_scalar`, `setup_downforce*`), and duplicate `<driver tracks="Track_Id">` entries for per-round form. The player's own entry is included so car-performance scalars apply to their car.
5. **Checklist for the player**: class, track/layout, laps, date/weather, "set AI opponents = N". Then they race in-game.
6. **Result capture** (three tiers, one result model): primary = in-app `$pcars2$` shared-memory poller (last-valid-frame snapshot on `mRaceState → RACESTATE_INVALID` after `SESSION_RACE`; custom AI names propagate into `mName`, ported from CrewChiefV4/AMS2_SessionLogger); secondary = FileSystemWatcher on `Documents\SecondMonitor\Reports` JSONs; tertiary = **manual entry as a first-class flow** (drag-order grid, DNF toggles) — never a shameful fallback, since aggregate-time races and missed sessions require it. Every capture lands on a confirm/correct screen with name→entry matching (exact, then fuzzy), then the raw payload is archived and the points engine recomputes standings.

## MVP → Later

**MVP = the season manager loop** (create career from pack → generate AI XML → race → capture → era-correct standings → next round → next season), with one polished bundled reference pack (1988, F-Classic Gen2, referencing the OverTake skin pack) proving the format. Minimal career continuity (persistent player profile, season archive) ships in MVP so saves are future-proof for the tycoon layer.

**Later = the tycoon differentiator** (finances, contracts, reputation, aging/retirement — the gap no active competitor fills), broader capture options, pack tooling, and non-F1 series. Phasing this way gets a useful tool shipped before Reiza's own 2026 career mode lands, while the pack format and career DB are designed from day one to carry the tycoon sim.

## Key risks
See structured risk list — headline items: Reiza's official career mode (late 2026), game-update churn in class names/shared-memory struct (mitigated by the updatable bundled library + pinned packs + manual entry), livery-name binding fragility (mitigated by preflight scan), and tycoon scope creep (mitigated by the MVP cut above).
mvp: WPF app on .NET 10 LTS, single self-contained exe (PublishSingleFile), MVVM with logic isolated in a UI-free Core library
SQLite career save file (one .ams2career per career, WAL mode, versioned migrations) with raw result payloads archived for recompute
Season pack format v1: JSON folders under Documents, JSON Schema validation on import, packs immutable and pinned (copied+hashed) into the career DB at season start

Pack schema covering series/season/calendar (AMS2 track xml_name binding, laps/distance, non-championship rounds, guest entries, fallback tracks), entry list (teams/drivers/entries with rounds ranges, exact case-sensitive ams2LiveryName binding, AI ratings, per-team performance scalars), and data-driven points rules

Pure points/standings engine: drivers + constructors, era points tables, best-N whole and split-season drops, shared-drive split/zero, half points (legacy + 2022 sliding scale), constructors best-car-only, guest/excluded entries, fractional points via rational arithmetic, countback tiebreaks
f1db-based oracle test suite replaying official 1950-2026 standings against the engine in CI

Custom-AI XML generator: Steam install auto-detect, per-class file to UserData\CustomAIDrivers, timestamped backup/restore of existing files (NAMeS-safe), UTF-8/escaping, per-track override entries, performance scalars
Preflight validation: class filename + livery names checked against bundled vehicle/livery/track library and a scan of installed CustomLiveries override XMLs; grid-size warnings
Race Day checklist screen (class, track/layout, laps, weather, AI count) driving the manual in-game race setup
Result capture tier 1: in-app $pcars2$ shared-memory reader with end-of-session snapshot and confirm/correct screen (name-to-entry matching)
Result capture tier 3 (also MVP): first-class manual result entry (drag-order grid, DNF/DSQ toggles)
Season flow UI: calendar, standings with dropped-score display, race history, season archive
Basic multi-season continuity: persistent player profile, completed-season history, start-next-season flow
One bundled reference season pack (1988 F1, F-Classic_Gen2) referencing the existing OverTake skin pack
later: Tycoon economy: team budgets, prize money, sponsors, staff, team bankruptcy and grid attrition
Driver market: contract offers, negotiations, reputation system, losing/earning seats
Aging, retirement, generated rookie drivers, multi-decade era progression (Vintage through Hybrid classes)
Result capture tier 2: Second Monitor JSON report watcher (lap-by-lap data, gaps, sector times, pit stops) feeding richer stats
Dynamic AI development: form curves, per-season rating drift, career-sim-driven round overrides written into the XML
Season pack creator/editor GUI and in-app pack browser/downloader (community repository)
Non-F1 series packs: F-USA/CART, GT, Group C, stock car, junior ladder (karts to F1 career paths)
Ladder/license career progression and cross-era or alternate-history careers
AMS2CM integration and deeper skin-pack detection/installation assistance
CREST2-style local HTTP endpoint or companion overlay for second-screen live timing
Avalonia port if cross-platform demand materializes (Core/Data/Ams2 layers are UI-agnostic by design)
Localization, theming, and community translation of packs
risks: Reiza's official AMS2 career mode (targeted late 2026) could absorb demand; differentiation must stay on historical eras, era-correct scoring, and the tycoon economy none of the competitors (Rewind GP, Race Pace) ship

Game updates rename vehicle classes and bump the shared-memory struct version (v1.6.9 renames and SHARED_MEMORY_VERSION=14 are precedents); mitigated by shipping the class/livery/track library as updatable data, pinning packs into careers, and keeping manual entry as a capture fallback

Livery-name binding is inherently fragile (case-sensitive exact match, special characters, unescaped ampersands in community skin packs); preflight scanning reduces but cannot eliminate user-side mismatch failures
Writing into the Steam game-install directory may hit ACL/permission issues on some setups and can clobber other tools' XMLs (NAMeS, XML Selector packs); backup/restore must be bulletproof
Second Monitor JSON schema is app-internal and unversioned; shared-memory capture misses any session where the app was not running

IP exposure: distributing real driver/team names and referencing skin packs of real liveries sits in the same gray zone as the existing modding scene; f1db seeding requires CC BY attribution; McLaren license doubt may delist cars that bundled packs reference
Scope creep of the tycoon simulation delaying the shippable season-manager MVP; the phase cut must be enforced
Performance scalars in custom-AI files also affect the player's car, which is used as a feature here but may surprise users and complicate fair AI-difficulty calibration
Solo/low-headcount delivery risk: the f1db oracle suite and pure-Core architecture are the main defenses against regression cost as eras are added

ux-workflow.proposal:
# AMS2 Career Companion — UX Design Proposal

## 0. Design principles

1. **The loop is the product.** Briefing → race in AMS2 → result entry repeats 16–30× per season. Every screen in the loop must be usable with keyboard only, readable at a glance, and never require scrolling for the happy path. Everything else (career, news, browser) is allowed to be "slow UI."
2. **The app never pretends to control AMS2.** It *stages* (writes the CustomAIDrivers XML) and *instructs* (a checklist mirroring AMS2's own custom-race setup screen order). Trust is built by the checklist matching the game UI verbatim — exact in-game class names, track/layout names, copyable strings.
3. **Two-state home.** When a career is active the app opens directly on Race Day, which has exactly two modes: **Briefing** (before the race) and **Enter Result** (after). No hunting for "where do I click."
4. **Data in, drama out.** The tycoon/narrative layer consumes only what the loop already captures (finishing order, DNFs, fastest lap, standings deltas). No extra input is ever demanded to make the career feel alive.
5. **Manual entry is a first-class flow, not a fallback.** Auto-capture (shared memory / Second Monitor JSON) is designed as a *pre-fill* of the same confirmation screen, so both paths converge on one result model and one UI.

---

## 1. App shell & navigation

Single window, 1280×800 minimum, dark theme with a per-era accent palette (1967 = cream/racing green serif masthead; 1988 = bold red/white; 2005 = glossy blue). Left rail, top-to-bottom:

```
┌──────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ▶ RACE DAY   ← primary, shows "R7 • Monaco" badge    │
│ Standings                                             │
│ Career        ← dot badge when offers/news unread     │
│ Season        (calendar, entry list, rules)           │
│ History       (past seasons, scrapbook)               │
│ Browser       (eras & season packs)                   │
│ ⚙ Settings                                            │
└──────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Global keys: `F1` Briefing, `F2` Enter Result, `F3` Standings, `F4` Career hub. `Ctrl+N` new career. The rail collapses to icons below 1100px width.

**Core loop state machine:** `Briefing (round N)` → user races in AMS2 → `Enter Result (round N)` → `Confirm` → optional interstitial (news card / offer, max one screen, dismiss with Enter) → `Briefing (round N+1)`. Season end replaces the interstitial with the Season Review flow.

---

## 2. Screen: Pre-Race Briefing ("check once and go")

One panel, zero scrolling, laid out in the same order as AMS2's custom race setup screen so the user can mirror it top-to-bottom with the game on the other monitor / alt-tabbed.

```
┌─ ROUND 7 / 16 · MONACO GRAND PRIX · 15 May 1988 ────────────────┐
│  SET UP IN AMS2 (Custom Race)                                    │
│  Class      F-Classic Gen2                        [copy]         │
│  Track      Azure Circuit                         [copy]         │
│  Race       78 laps  (100%)  · or 39 (50% — your setting)        │
│  Opponents  25   ·  AI skill 96 / aggression Medium (your cal.)  │
│  Weather    Clear · Real date · Rolling start OFF (1988 rules)   │
│                                                                   │
│  STAGED BY THE APP                                                │
│  ✓ F-Classic_Gen2.xml   26 drivers · written 14:02  [Restage]    │
│    incl. round-7 form + street-specialist track override          │
│  ✓ Skin pack detected: [AMS2] F1 1988 Season v3.1                │
│  ⚠ Grid note: Senna/Prost turbo cars = Model 3 (MP4/4 via DLC)   │
│                                                                   │
│  Storyline: Berger leads you by 6 pts · Beat him for P2 in WDC   │
│  Team says: finish P4 or better (contract objective)              │
│                                                                   │
│  [ I'M RACING → ]                  [ Skip round ] [ Edit round ]  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Details that kill friction:

- **Stage on arrival.** The XML for the round (round-specific entry list, per-driver ratings incl. current form, per-track overrides) is written automatically when the briefing opens; the ✓ line just confirms it, with timestamp. `Restage` is for users who run other tools (NAMeS, XML Selector) that may have overwritten the file. A file watcher flips ✓→⚠ live if another program touches the class XML.
- **Copy buttons** put exact strings ("Azure Circuit", "F-Classic Gen2") on the clipboard for users searching in-game lists.
- **"Your setting" memory.** Race length %, AI skill and aggression are remembered per career; the panel shows the resolved numbers, not preferences to re-decide.
- **Substitute-track banner.** If the pack maps a missing venue to a substitute (e.g. 1988 Suzuka → Kansai Classic), show it plainly: "Standing in for Suzuka."
- **One storyline line + one objective line.** That's the entire narrative injection pre-race — enough to give the race stakes, never a wall of text.
- `I'M RACING →` marks the round in-progress and minimizes to tray (optional setting). When the app regains focus, Race Day is already in Enter Result mode.

Target time on this screen: **under 15 seconds** once the user trusts the staging.

## 3. Screen: Post-Race Result Entry (the 90-second screen)

### Layout

```
┌─ ENTER RESULT · R7 Monaco ── timer 0:34 ─────────────────────────┐
│  P1: pro_                          │ REMAINING (14)               │
│                                    │  2 Berger   27 Nannini       │
│  1  #12 SENNA        McLaren       │  3 Boutsen  28 Cheever  ...  │
│  2  #11 PROST ◀      McLaren       │ (numbers, 3-letter codes,    │
│  3  …                              │  dims as drivers are placed) │
│  YOU are #17 — not yet placed      │                              │
│──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────│
│ type name/# + Enter · Ctrl+↑↓ nudge · F8 DNF phase · Ctrl+Z undo │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Keyboard grammar

- **One input stream.** Focus never leaves the entry box. Type **car number** (`12⏎`) or **2–3 letters** of surname (`sen⏎`) — type-ahead resolves against *unplaced* drivers only, so ambiguity shrinks every entry. Single unambiguous match auto-selects; ties show a 2–3 item picker navigated with ↑↓.
- **Numbers are the speed path.** Entry lists show permanent car numbers; users who note the AMS2 results screen by number can do ~3 keystrokes per car.
- **Player shortcut:** `me⏎` or `*⏎` places the player at the current position.
- **DNF phase (`F8` or just stop placing):** the classified order is done; remaining drivers appear as a checklist. `↑↓` + `D` marks DNF, optional one-letter reason (`m`echanical, `a`ccident, `s`pin, `o`ther) and optional lap number — both skippable with Enter. **Bulk close:** "Mark all 6 remaining as DNF?" one Enter. Reasons matter for reliability narrative but are never required.
- **Fastest lap:** one type-ahead field, shown only if the active points system or stats care (1950s F1, 2019–24, or stats-only). Skippable.
- **Corrections:** `Ctrl+↑/↓` nudges the highlighted row; mouse drag-reorder works too; `Ctrl+Z` unlimited undo; `Esc` clears the current token.
- **Penalties (post-order):** highlight driver → `X` = DSQ (excluded, others promote automatically), `T` = position adjustment (asks "+N places"), `P` = points penalty (noted, applied in standings). Rare, so hidden behind single keys rather than persistent UI.
- **Quali:** collapsed by default. One optional field: "Your grid position: __". Full quali-order entry is an opt-in second pass for purists (identical grammar), never blocking.

### Timing budget (26-car grid, 90-second target)
- 15 classified finishers × ~3.5s (glance at AMS2 results + 3 keystrokes) ≈ 55s
- DNF bulk phase ≈ 15s
- Fastest lap + confirm ≈ 10s → **~80s**, with a soft on-screen timer as a fun self-competition, not a nag.

### Confirm screen = the payoff

Confirmation is not a modal chore; it's the dopamine frame: computed points for the round, **animated standings delta** ("You: P4 → P3 ▲, +3 pts on Berger"), team objective met/missed, and one generated headline. `Enter` commits. Results remain editable later from Season view (with an "amended" marker), so users never fear committing.

### Auto-capture (later phase, same screen)

Shared-memory listener ($pcars2$) or Second Monitor JSON watcher pre-fills the entire order; the user lands directly on the confirm screen with a "captured automatically — review" banner. Manual grammar remains available to fix capture errors. One result model, two importers, one UI.

---

## 4. Screens: Standings & Season Review

**Standings (F3)** — two tabs + matrix:
- **Drivers:** era-correct points with dropped-scores transparency: `36 (41)` = counted (gross), tooltip lists which results are dropped under e.g. "best 11 of 16". Player row pinned/highlighted.
- **Constructors:** respects era rules (exists only from 1958; best-car-only pre-1980). Hidden entirely for pre-1958 seasons rather than showing an empty table.
- **Round matrix:** drivers × rounds grid, cells colored by finish (gold/silver/bronze/points/DNF-red/DNS-grey) — the classic Wikipedia results table, instantly legible, zero interaction needed. Horizontal scroll inside the table only.
- **Rules chip** in the header: "1988 · 9-6-4-3-2-1 · best 11 count" → popover explains the season's full scoring quirks in plain language. This is where era-correctness becomes *visible* rather than a black box.
- Late-season: a "title picture" strip ("Senna clinches at Suzuka if he wins and Prost is ≤2nd") — later phase, needs permutation math.

**Season Review (end of season)** — a generated, era-styled "Season Annual": front-page masthead, champions, superlatives (most wins/poles/DNFs, biggest upset), the player's season grade vs contract objectives, 5-line narrative recap stitched from the season's stored headlines, and the final matrix. One page, exportable as PNG for sharing. Then flows into the off-season sequence (offers → contract signing → next-season pack selection).

---

## 5. Screen: Career Hub (F4)

A dashboard of cards — read-only glances, each expanding to a detail view:

- **Contract card:** team, role (#1/#2), salary, expiry, objective progress bar ("Top 4 in WDC — currently P3 ✓").
- **Reputation:** two simple gauges — *Pace* and *Reliability/Professionalism* — plus a per-team relationship list. Deliberately not a 12-stat RPG sheet.
- **Finances:** driver-side ledger (salary, prize money, win bonuses; era-scaled currency). In team-owner mode (later), this card becomes the tycoon panel: budget, sponsor income, car development spend, bankruptcy risk meter — and AI teams run the same sim in the background so backmarkers can fold mid-era even in driver careers.
- **Offers inbox:** contract offers rendered as era-appropriate correspondence (1960s telegram, 1980s fax, 2000s email) — pure CSS skinning, huge charm, zero content cost. Accept/decline/negotiate (negotiation = one counter-offer roll, later phase).
- **News feed:** reverse-chron headline list (see §8), capped at ~3 items per round.
- **Career timeline:** horizontal strip of seasons — team color, final position, championships starred. Click into any past season's frozen standings/annual (History section).

## 6. Flow: New-Career Wizard (5 steps, all skippable to defaults)

1. **Pick season** — embedded era/season browser (§7) filtered by "content you own" toggle. Selecting a season shows its readiness score immediately.
2. **Verify content** — automated scan, one line per requirement with ✓/✗/⚠:
   - Game class installed (base/DLC — e.g. "Formula HiTech DLC required" with Steam link)
   - Track layouts (exact / substitute / missing-with-substitute-proposed)
   - Skin pack detected in `Documents\...\CustomLiveries\Overrides` (fingerprint match against the pack manifest; link to OverTake page if missing)
   - Write access to `<install>\UserData\CustomAIDrivers\` (folder created if absent; detects Steam path automatically, manual browse fallback)
   - "Proceed anyway" is always available: missing skins degrade to stock liveries with fictional-livery mapping; nothing hard-blocks.
3. **Pick your seat** — team grid sorted by car competitiveness tier (labelled honestly: Frontrunner / Midfield / Backmarker, with expected finishing range). Choose to **replace a historical driver** (take Nannini's Benetton seat) or **add yourself** as an extra entry (grid grows by one). Enter name, nationality, helmet color chip.
4. **Difficulty & rules** — AI skill self-assessment (slider with plain-language anchors: "I race AI at ~95%"), race length %, and period-correct rule toggles pre-checked: dropped scores, shared-drive policy, constructor scoring mode, reliability attrition on/off. A single "Period-correct (recommended)" preset collapses this step to one click.
5. **Summary → Start career** — one card recap; lands on Round 1 Briefing.

Target: under 3 minutes for a user whose packs are already installed.

## 7. Screen: Era / Season Browser

- **Era shelf:** horizontal cards from Formula Vintage (1966–70) to F-Hybrid Gen3 (2022+), each with era artwork treatment, the AMS2 class badge, and year chips. Non-F1 series (F-USA/CART, Group C, DTM, Stock Car…) live under a second shelf, "Other Series."
- **Season page:** calendar list (round, date, venue, layout mapping status: ✓ exact 1988 layout / ≈ substitute / ✗ missing), entry-list preview (teams, drivers, car-model mapping e.g. "Lotus → F-Classic Gen2 Model 3"), required-content manifest with install-state icons and links (DLC on Steam, packs on OverTake), and the season's scoring rules chip.
- **Readiness score** per season ("14/16 rounds exact, skins installed, DLC owned → 96% authentic") — turns content checking into collection satisfaction.
- CTAs: **Start Career here** / **Run as standalone season** (championship tracker without the tycoon layer — important for users who just want era-correct standings).
- **Import pack…** accepts a season-pack zip/folder (JSON manifest + optional custom-AI baseline), validates, and shelves it. Packs *reference* existing OverTake skinpacks rather than bundling textures.

## 8. Living career without bloat

- **Headlines, not prose.** After each round, generate 1–3 headlines from a seeded template bank keyed on events already captured (winner, player delta, championship swing, notable DNF, rivalry beat): "NANNINI STUNS McLARENS IN MONACO RAIN". Deterministic seed per round → reproducible, testable, no AI-runtime cost.
- **Rivalry engine (later):** rivals emerge from data — repeated finishes within 1 position, same team, or championship adjacency — and get a persistent tracker chip ("You vs Berger: 4–3 this season") that headlines can reference. One active rivalry surfaced at a time.
- **Era skinning as narrative.** Typography, masthead, offer-letter styling per era do most of the atmospheric work for zero ongoing content cost.
- **The scrapbook.** Every headline, annual, and contract is stored on the career timeline — the career *accumulates* visibly, which is what makes 10 seasons feel like a life. Nothing is ever shown twice or demands acknowledgment beyond one Enter.
- **Hard bloat rules:** no interstitial longer than one screen; max one narrative beat pre-race and three post-race; every narrative surface dismissible with Enter; a "minimal narrative" toggle turns all of it off for pure-tracker users.

## 9. Cross-cutting UX requirements

- Startup < 1s to Race Day; all state autosaved (SQLite/JSON, single user-data folder); full undo/edit of any past result with recomputed standings; every list keyboard-navigable; copy-to-clipboard on all in-game-string fields; no network required at runtime (pack links open browser); single portable exe.

## 10. Phasing summary

**MVP** proves the loop: wizard → briefing/staging → 90-second entry → era-correct standings → light career (rep, offers, salary, aging/retirement) → annual recap, shipping with 2–3 curated F1 season packs and the pack format documented. **Later** adds auto-capture, the team-finance tycoon, rivalries/title-math, non-F1 series, the pack creator, and performance-scalar staging.
mvp: App shell with Race Day two-state home (Briefing / Enter Result), left-rail nav, F1-F4 global keys, per-era accent theming (basic)

New-career wizard: season pick, automated content verification (Steam path detection, CustomAIDrivers write check, skin-pack fingerprint scan, DLC/track mapping status) with proceed-anyway fallbacks, seat selection (replace driver or add self), period-correct rules preset

Pre-race briefing panel: AMS2-setup-order checklist with exact in-game names + copy buttons, remembered race-length/AI-skill settings, automatic per-round CustomAIDrivers XML staging with file-watch status, substitute-track banners, one storyline + one objective line
Round-specific custom AI XML generation: per-round entry lists, driver ratings with simple form variation, per-track overrides, UTF-8/escaping/case-sensitive livery-name safety

Keyboard-first result entry: single input stream with number and surname type-ahead against unplaced drivers, player shortcut, DNF phase with bulk-confirm and optional one-letter reasons, fastest-lap field (rule-aware), DSQ/position/points penalties via single keys, Ctrl+Z undo, drag reorder fallback, editable-after-commit results
Confirm screen with computed round points, animated standings delta, objective check, one generated headline

Era-correct points engine as data-driven rule objects: points tables per era, dropped scores/best-N (incl. split-half seasons), shared-drive policy, constructor best-car-only and 1958+ existence, fractional points, manual half-points toggle; validated against f1db standings
Standings views: drivers (counted vs gross points), constructors (era-aware), round-by-round color matrix, scoring-rules explainer chip
Season Review annual page: champions, superlatives, player grade vs objectives, 5-line recap, exportable PNG

Light career sim: reputation from results, season-end contract offers, AI driver aging/retirement, salary and prize money
Headline generator: seeded template bank, 1-3 headlines per round, minimal-narrative toggle
Era/season browser with readiness score, required-content manifest and links, Start Career / standalone-season tracker modes
Season-pack format (JSON manifest referencing existing OverTake skinpacks + custom-AI baselines) with import/validation; 2-3 curated F1 packs bundled (e.g. 1975, 1988, 1991)
Single portable exe, offline-first, autosave, sub-second startup, full keyboard navigation
later: Auto result capture: in-app $pcars2$ shared-memory listener with end-of-session snapshot, plus Second Monitor JSON report watcher — both pre-filling the existing confirm screen
Team-finance tycoon layer: budgets, sponsors, car development spend, bankruptcy (player and background-simulated AI teams), team-owner mode reusing the finances card
Rivalry engine with persistent tracker chips and rivalry-aware headlines
Title-permutation calculator and late-season 'clinch scenarios' strip
Full quali-order entry pass and richer session stats (poles, front-row counts)
Difficulty calibration from lap times and season-over-season AI skill auto-tuning (form, aging feeding XML values)

risks: Reiza's official AMS2 career mode (targeted end of 2026) could absorb the casual audience — the app must differentiate on historical eras, era-correct scoring, and tycoon depth, which Reiza is unlikely to cover

Livery-name binding fragility: custom-AI staging silently fails if livery_name doesn't exactly match installed skin pack names (case, special characters, pack version drift) — mitigated by fingerprint scanning and pre-race file watch, but user pack heterogeneity guarantees edge cases
Game updates rename classes and bump the shared-memory struct version (v1.6.9 renames already happened); class/track/livery libraries must be data files updatable independently of the exe

Manual entry fatigue over 16-30 rounds/season is the core retention risk; if the 90-second target slips in practice (e.g. users can't read AMS2's results screen by car number), the loop breaks — needs early usability testing with real grids

Competitor overlap: Rewind GP already ships historical F1 careers with auto-capture and season packs; shipping MVP without auto-capture concedes a visible feature gap even if manual-first is deliberate

Era scoring engine correctness (split-half dropped scores, shared drives, Indy 500 exclusions) is a credibility feature for the target audience; errors are highly visible — must be unit-tested against f1db official standings for every supported season

Writing into the Steam game-install directory (UserData\CustomAIDrivers) can hit permission issues (Program Files ACLs, non-standard Steam libraries) and conflicts with other tools (NAMeS, XML Selector) overwriting the same per-class XML

Season packs referencing third-party OverTake skinpacks depend on external hosting and pack authors' continued maintenance (e.g. McLaren license doubt may delist MP4-era content), so readiness checks must degrade gracefully

Scope creep on the tycoon/narrative layer threatens the 'lightweight single exe' promise; the bloat rules (one-screen interstitials, minimal-narrative toggle) need enforcement discipline through later phases
